Processor call times out — charged or not? Never guess: same-key idempotent retry, park in pending_confirmation (a legitimate state), resolve by webhook-or-poll, reconcile nightly. Payments

Feature flags: the three-layer fail-static stack?

Last-known ruleset in memory → signed CDN snapshot on restart → compiled-in defaults. "Yesterday's behavior, never chaos." Feature flags

docs.title: The Framework
docs.text: Forty-five minutes, one open-ended prompt, and a rubric you can't see. The framework below is the standard seven-phase structure with the timing that fits, plus — more valuable — what each phase is actually scoring and the recovery plays for when it wobbles. Run it enough times (practice page) and it stops being a checklist and becomes what it is for great candidates: the shape their thinking naturally takes. It's the same skill as running an incident: structure that survives pressure.

docs.title: The seven phases (45-minute calibration)
docs.text:
1. Requirements — 5 minutes. Scope ruthlessly: functional ("which 3 features are core? I'll explicitly park the rest"), non-functional (scale, latency targets, consistency needs per data class, availability tier), and the constraints question that flavors everything ("read-heavy or write-heavy? global or regional?"). Scoring: do you navigate ambiguity or charge into it? The strongest single move: end the phase by restating the agreed scope aloud — it converts a fuzzy prompt into a contract, and interviewers remember who did that. (Full technique page.)

2. Estimation — 3 minutes. The recipes: QPS, storage, bandwidth — ending with the verdict: "so the hard part is X." Estimation exists to aim the interview; a number without a consequence is theater. Skip the full ceremony when scale is given — but always do enough arithmetic to justify where you'll spend your time.

3. API & data model — 5 minutes. The core entities, their key structure (the shard key decision starts here), and the 3–5 endpoints/operations with idempotency noted where it matters. This is the gap phase for ops-background candidates — do not rush it; interviewers specifically check whether infrastructure people can model data.

4. High-level design — 10 minutes. The skeleton: stores, flows, transformations — boxes with jobs, arrows with protocols, and the sync/async boundary drawn explicitly. Narrate as you draw; end with a full request traced through ("a write enters here, lands there, fans out to..."). Scoring: coherence — does the shape serve the requirements from phase 1? Resist detail — depth is the next phase's job, and diving early is the classic time-trap.

5. Deep dives — 15 minutes. The interview is won here. Two or three components, chosen by where the hard part lives (your phase-2 verdict) and steered by the interviewer's interest. This is where the whole site deploys: the four questions on your own design, the bottleneck sequence ("at 10× this breaks first..."), the named trade-offs with the template. Let the interviewer redirect you — their interruptions are the rubric talking.

6. Operability — 4 minutes, your signature. The three moves, compressed: "Observability: RED per service, queue age, trace propagation end-to-end. Rollout: flag-gated, canary by cell, auto-rollback on burn. Failure story: the two scariest boxes and their degraded modes — and the failover is game-day'd." Most candidates don't have this phase at all; yours should be rehearsed to fluency.

7. Wrap — 3 minutes. Bottlenecks recap, what you'd build first vs. defer (the staged honesty), and the trade-offs you'd revisit with more information. Ending with judgment — not apology — is the last impression that sticks.

docs.title: Recovery plays (the part nobody rehearses)
docs.text:
• Blank on a component → zoom out to first principles: "I haven't built this exact thing; reasoning from properties: it needs ordered delivery per key, so something log-shaped..." Deriving beats remembering — and interviewers prefer watching derivation.
  • Caught in an error → the estimation-recovery move generalized: name it, fix it, move on. "Actually, that's wrong — at this write rate the cache-aside race matters; let me fix the invalidation." Self-correction is positive signal; defended errors are fatal.
  • Interviewer pushes back → treat it as a design review gift: steel-man their concern first ("you're right that the fan-out explodes at celebrity scale"), then either adapt or respectfully defend with a named trade-off. The worst response is agreeing without changing anything — that's neither conviction nor flexibility.
  • Time collapsing → say the prioritization aloud: "ten minutes left — I'd rather deep-dive the hot-partition story than polish the API; sound right?" Managing the clock visibly is itself senior signal.
  • The mid-interview twist ("now make it multi-region") → it's not sabotage, it's the rubric's depth probe; ask why first, then adapt the design incrementally, narrating what changes and what survives. Rigid designs that can't absorb twists score worse than rougher designs that bend.

Staff+ altitude

The framework is the same at every level; the altitude of each phase changes (the full level map): requirements gain a "should this exist / what's the simplest thing that works" beat; estimation gains cost verdicts alongside scale verdicts; high-level design gains explicit evolution ("v1 is a modular monolith with the partition key pre-planned; here's the seam we split at 10×" — the staged-and-costed instinct); deep dives gain organizational trade-offs (this boundary is a team boundary); and the wrap gains a risk register ("the bet I'm least sure of is X; here's how we'd find out cheaply"). At Principal altitude, expect fewer boxes and more whether: build-vs-buy, sequencing, what you'd refuse to build. The framework holds; the sentences inside it grow up.

In the interview

Meta-advice for the framework itself: announce it — thirty seconds up front ("I'll clarify scope, size it, sketch the data model and API, draw the high level, then spend most of our time deep in the hard parts — steer me anytime") buys enormous structural credit and permission to manage the clock; timestamp your transitions ("that's the high level — with 20 minutes left, let's go deep on the fan-out") so drift is impossible; and let phase 5 breathe — the framework's biggest failure mode is spending 30 minutes on phases 1–4 and leaving the actual scoring phase 8 minutes. The time split to internalize: one-third setup, two-thirds depth. And remember the exception rule: if the interviewer redirects, follow — the framework serves the conversation, never the reverse.

Next: Requirements & estimation — the two setup phases, upgraded from ritual to weapon.

docs.title: Question Bank & Practice
docs.text: The canon of system design questions is smaller than it looks — a few dozen prompts, most of which are the same five or six underlying shapes wearing different products. This page maps the canon to this site's case studies, names the shapes so you can recognize a "new" question as an old one in costume, and lays out a practice regimen that builds the actual skill: performing design out loud, under a clock.

docs.title: The canon, mapped
docs.text:
The classics (asked everywhere, at every level):

Question Case study The shape underneath URL shortener full walkthrough Key generation, read-heavy caching, redirects Rate limiter full walkthrough Algorithms + distributed state Notification system full walkthrough Queue fan-out, providers, idempotency Chat (WhatsApp/Slack) full walkthrough Stateful connections, per-key ordering, presence News feed (Twitter/Instagram) full walkthrough Push/pull fan-out, the celebrity problem Video streaming (YouTube/Netflix) full walkthrough Object storage + CDN + pipeline Typeahead / autocomplete full walkthrough Prefix structures, cached top-K Web crawler full walkthrough Frontier queue, politeness, dedup Ride-hailing / proximity (Uber) full walkthrough Geo-indexing, matching, location ingest Payment system full walkthrough Ledgers, sagas, reconciliation Key-value store (Dynamo-style) full walkthrough The NoSQL page as a design

The DevOps specials (increasingly asked, and your home turf — the full argument):

Question Case study Why it favors you Metrics/monitoring system full walkthrough You've operated the real one Log aggregation pipeline full walkthrough The volume math is your daily bread CI/CD platform full walkthrough Queue-DAG-artifact-trust, lived Distributed job scheduler full walkthrough Leases, fencing, exactly-once-ish Feature flag system full walkthrough Config planes and fail-static

The shapes — recognize these and no question is truly new: read-heavy content (shortener, feeds, video, typeahead → cache/CDN/precompute); write-heavy ingest (metrics, logs, tracking → partition/batch/append); fan-out (feeds, notifications, chat → push/pull + amplification math); stateful connections (chat, gaming, collaboration → connection tier + pub/sub backbone); coordination-critical (schedulers, payments, inventory → idempotency + ownership + fencing); matching/geo (ride-hailing, delivery → spatial index + streaming location). When a prompt lands, your first silent question is which shapes is this made of? — most real products are two shapes stapled together (Instagram = read-heavy content + fan-out; Uber Eats = matching + fan-out).

docs.title: The practice regimen
docs.text:
Knowledge accumulates by reading; the performance skill builds only one way — out loud, timed, repeatedly:

  1. Study phase (per case study): read the walkthrough actively — pause at each design decision, answer before reading on, run the four questions on each component yourself.
  2. Rehearsal phase: 48 hours later, take the same prompt cold — 40 minutes, whiteboard or tablet, speaking continuously to an empty room. The silence where your narration stalls is the exact spot to re-study. (Recording and reviewing one of these per week is the communication page's highest-ROI exercise.)
  3. Transfer phase: take an unmapped prompt (design Strava, Ticketmaster, a parking system, Google Docs) and run the full framework — the test is whether the shapes transfer, and ticket-buying (inventory + contention + the thundering herd on drop day) or collaborative editing (CRDTs/OT + presence) will tell you honestly.
  4. Mock phase: humans, ideally strangers — peers, mock platforms, or the strongest engineer who'll say yes. Ask them to interrupt, redirect, and push back mid-flow; handling the twist is a skill reading cannot build. Two mocks minimum before the real loop; the first one is always rough, which is precisely why it shouldn't be the real one.

Cadence for a 3–4 week runway: week 1 — Foundations + framework + two classics studied-and-rehearsed; week 2 — four more case studies (mix classics and specials) + first mock; week 3 — transfer prompts + deep-dive weak sections (the rehearsal stalls tell you which) + second mock; final days — cheat sheets, re-rehearse your two strongest walkthroughs (confidence is a legitimate prep target), sleep.

Staff+ altitude

Adjust practice to the loop you're actually facing: Staff+ loops add design-review interviews (critique a flawed design — practice by running the four questions and the review lenses against sample architectures, out loud), deep retrospectives ("walk me through the hardest system you've operated" — structure your war stories as detection → decision → systemic fix, and have three ready: an outage, a migration, a design you reversed), and the whether-questions (build-vs-buy, sequencing, kill decisions) — practice by taking any canon prompt and answering only the Principal layer for ten minutes: what would you buy, defer, refuse? If that layer feels thin, the Staff+ callouts across this site are your syllabus — they were written to be exactly that.

In the interview

Question-selection meta, for when loops offer choice or interviewers ask "what would you like to design?": pick the prompt whose shape you've rehearsed, not the product you use most — familiarity with the app is worth far less than fluency with the pattern. And if you're offered a DevOps special, take it every time: the metrics system or CI/CD platform prompt hands you home-field advantage that no generic candidate can match — you'll be describing systems you've operated in anger, and that smell is the strongest signal you can emit. The site's closing advice for the night before: don't cram new material — re-read the DevOps lens and your two rehearsed walkthroughs, review the cheat sheets, and remember that the interviewer is hoping you're good. Go be what they're hoping for.

Next: the Case Studies themselves — every walkthrough above, in full.

docs.title: Requirements & Estimation
docs.text: The first eight minutes of the interview decide what the remaining thirty-seven are about — and most candidates spend them on autopilot, asking generic questions ("how many users?") and doing ceremonial math they never use. This page upgrades both phases from ritual to weapon: requirements as scope control, estimation as decision-making. (The core estimation toolkit — numbers, recipes, worked examples — lives in Foundations; this page is its interview deployment.)

docs.title: Requirements: scope is the deliverable
docs.text:
The prompt is deliberately oversized — "design WhatsApp" is six months of work. The graded skill is cutting it down defensibly:

Functional scope — the core-three move. Enumerate what the product could do, then cut: "WhatsApp is 1:1 chat, group chat, presence, media, calls, stories. Core: 1:1 delivery, group fan-out, online/offline presence. I'll park media behind the presigned-URL pattern in one sentence and skip calls entirely — flag if you want those." That paragraph demonstrates product sense, the courage to defer, and gives the interviewer a cheap steering moment — all in twenty seconds.

Non-functional scope — the questions that change the design. Not a checklist recital; ask the 3–4 whose answers reshape architecture, and know why you're asking (each maps to a site section):

Question Because it decides Read:write ratio? The entire architecture class — cache-heavy vs. ingest-shaped Scale now, and in 18 months? Which rungs of the ladder you design for vs. pre-plan Latency target, and for which operations? The budget, sync/async boundary, geography What must never be lost / never be wrong? Consistency per data class, durability posture Global or regional? Residency constraints? The multi-region ladder and homing Availability tier — what does downtime cost? Which nine, and what it buys

The per-data-class instinct is the differentiator: don't accept "strongly consistent" for the whole system — decompose it. "Messages: never lost, per-conversation ordered. Presence: approximate, seconds-stale is fine. Profiles: eventual." One sentence, three data classes, three different (cheaper, better) designs unlocked — and you've demonstrated the five-rows-one-application pattern before drawing a box.

Anti-patterns: interrogating for ten minutes (five questions maximum, then decide — comfort with ambiguity is itself scored); accepting the prompt silently (charging into "design Twitter" without scoping reads as junior no matter what follows); and re-litigating scope mid-interview (the phase-1 contract exists so phase-5 depth doesn't drown in "wait, do we support editing?").

docs.title: Estimation: arithmetic with a verdict
docs.text:
The foundations page taught the numbers and recipes; the interview deployment adds three disciplines:

Every calculation ends in a decision. The template: assumption → arithmetic → "so." "500M DAU × 20 views ÷ 10⁵ ≈ 100k QPS, 300k peak — so this is CDN territory and the origin design is almost boring; the real fight is the 100 PB of media — so object storage with tiering is where I'll spend our time." Numbers that don't steer the interview are stage props, and interviewers know the difference.

Estimate at decision points, not just up front. The opening sizing pass aims the interview; surgical estimation mid-design wins it: before choosing fan-out strategy — "average user: 200 followers, so a post = 200 timeline writes; celebrity: 100M, so push breaks and we go hybrid"; before choosing a cache size — "hot set is 20% of 500 GB = 100 GB — one Redis cluster, not a fleet." The reflex of reaching for arithmetic when a choice appears is the strongest estimation signal there is.

Know which verdicts recur. Interview problems cluster into about five estimation shapes: traffic-boring, storage-hard (media platforms → tiering and CDN economics); write-heavy ingest (metrics, logs, tracking → batch, partition, append); fan-out multiplication (feeds, notifications → the amplification trace); memory-fits check (rate limiters, sessions, presence → "it's 8 GB, one box, the hard part is elsewhere"); and connection-count (chat, gaming → FD/node limits → fleet size). Recognizing the shape in ten seconds is what practiced candidates do that first-timers can't.

DevOps lens

Your capacity-planning experience is this skill — say so through the numbers: when you estimate, anchor in operational reality ("a tuned API node does 5k RPS, so 300k peak is ~60 nodes plus headroom for the scale-out lag — call it 90"); when you scope non-functionals, ask the operator's versions ("what's the peak-to-average ratio? bursty ingest changes everything" — the queue-as-shock-absorber decision hangs on it); and deploy the derived-load instinct nobody else has: "every request also emits ~10 metrics samples and 2 log lines — at 300k RPS the telemetry pipeline is its own design problem." That last sentence, in a non-observability question, is pure differentiation.

Staff+ altitude

At Staff+, requirements gain a zeroth question: should this be built, and what's the simplest thing that could work? — "before designing a bespoke notification system: at this volume a managed push service + a queue covers v1; the custom fan-out tier earns itself at ~10× — I'll design for that but sequence it." Estimation gains cost verdicts alongside scale verdicts (the priced sentence: "~$0.002/user/month, egress-dominated — the CDN contract matters more than the compute design") and error bars as risk register ("the 2% upload assumption drives everything; if it's 10%, storage economics flip — I'd instrument that first"). And the requirements phase becomes bidirectional: Staff candidates volunteer constraints the interviewer forgot ("I'm assuming compliance wants EU residency — that homing decision is structural, so let's pin it now"). Asking better questions than the prompt anticipated is the level, demonstrated.

In the interview

The two phases compose into an opening you can rehearse as a unit — ninety seconds that sets up everything: scope contract (core three, parked features, flagged assumptions) → the 3 reshaping questions → sizing pass → the verdict sentence ("so the hard part is X, and that's where I'll spend our depth"). Practice it against five different prompts and the opening becomes free — zero cognitive load, maximum structural credit — leaving your full attention for the deep dives where the interview is won. One caution: interviewers sometimes answer your scoping questions with "you decide." That's not deflection; it's the test. Decide immediately, state the assumption, move — "then I'll assume 100M DAU, read-heavy 50:1, and single-region to start" — because comfort making calls under ambiguity is precisely what the phase measures.

Next: Communication & levels — the same design, narrated at Senior, Staff, and Principal altitude.

docs.title: Study Plans & Tracker
docs.text: Three plans for three runways, plus a progress tracker whose checkboxes persist in your browser (localStorage — tick them as you go; they'll be waiting when you return). Whichever plan you run: read actively, drill after each section, and rehearse out loud — the regimen is what converts reading into interview performance.

docs.title: The 48-hour cram
docs.text:
For the interview that's already scheduled. Ruthless triage — depth where it pays, callouts elsewhere.

  • Evening 1: The DevOps lens → Thinking in systems → Estimation (memorize the numbers table)
  • Evening 1: The framework — then announce it to an empty room twice
  • Day 2 morning: URL shortener + news feed, studied then rehearsed out loud, 40 min each
  • Day 2 afternoon: Cache failure modes, resilience patterns, delivery semantics — the three highest-yield follow-up pages
  • Day 2 evening: Drills — Foundations + case-study sections; re-drill misses
  • Morning of: Cheat sheets only. No new material. You know things.

docs.title: The 3-week interview sprint
docs.text:
The standard prep arc — one pass of everything, weighted toward performance.

  • Week 1 — foundations & data: all of Foundations (2 pages/day), then Data & Storage cram-priority order: replication, partitioning, storage engines, rest as reading
  • Week 1: Framework + requirements & estimation; rehearse the 90-second opening against 3 prompts
  • Week 2 — components & first blood: Caching, Messaging, Distributed Systems sections; Networking cram notes
  • Week 2: four case studies studied-and-rehearsed (shortener, rate limiter, feed, chat) + first mock
  • Week 3 — altitude & polish: Communication & levels (record yourself once), one DevOps special rehearsed (metrics or CI/CD), 2 transfer prompts cold (question bank)
  • Week 3: Observability SLO + alerting pages, deployments, multi-region — the operability-close ammunition
  • Week 3: second mock + drills full pass; final days per the cram plan

docs.title: The 12-week mastery track
docs.text:
The fall-in-love, Staff-trajectory path: everything, in order, with practice woven in. One section ≈ one week; the pace is deliberate — retention beats coverage.

  • Weeks 1–2: Foundations complete + framework; start the habit: one drill section per week, one estimation rep per day
  • Weeks 3–4: Networking + Data & Storage complete; rehearse shortener + KV store
  • Week 5: Caching + Messaging; rehearse rate limiter + notifications
  • Week 6: Distributed Systems complete — the fencing-token and metastability stories told out loud until fluent
  • Weeks 7–8: DevOps & Platform complete (your crown jewel — read every Staff+ callout twice); rehearse both CI/CD and scheduler specials
  • Week 9: Observability & SRE + Security; rehearse metrics system + log pipeline
  • Week 10: remaining case studies (feed, chat, video, proximity, payments); first mock
  • Week 11: Communication & levels deep work — record two sessions, run the Principal-layer-only exercise on three prompts; transfer prompts
  • Week 12: second + third mocks, drills full pass, weak-section re-reads; then one paper from resources per week, forever — that's the habit that compounds past the interview

docs.title: Leadership & Strategy (the Staff→CTO track)
docs.text:
• The path: Senior → CTO
  • Design docs, RFCs & ADRs
  • Architecture reviews
  • Technology strategy
  • Engineering org design
  • Executive craft
  • Three career stories written down (a system, a strategy, an org change) — why
  • One design doc written to the anatomy (real work, not practice)
  • One quarterly engineering-health one-pager shipped for your area — the exercise

docs.title: Leadership & Strategy
docs.text:
Everything before this section teaches you to design systems. This section teaches the layer around the systems — because Staff, Principal, and CTO are not "more system design": they're a change of medium. At Staff, your primary output becomes documents and reviews; at Principal, strategy — the few choices that make a thousand future choices easy; at CTO, the organization itself and the translation between technology and business. The technical mastery in the other twelve sections is the entry ticket; these six pages are the craft that spends it.

  • The path: Senior → Staff → Principal → CTO

    What actually changes at each level — scope, time horizon, artifacts, how impact is measured — and how to operate at the next level before you hold the title.

  • Design docs, RFCs & ADRs

    The single highest-leverage Staff+ skill: documents that make groups converge. Anatomy, alternatives-considered honesty, the RFC process, decision records.

  • Architecture reviews

    At Staff+ you review ten designs for every one you author. The consolidated review toolkit, feedback that lands, and killing projects kindly.

  • Technology strategy

    Diagnosis → policy → action. Innovation tokens, build-vs-buy, the tech radar, platform leverage, and migrations as the org-scale skill.

  • Engineering org design

    Conway's law as a tool: team topologies, cognitive load as the sizing metric, ownership models, and why every service boundary is a team proposal.

  • Executive craft

    The CTO layer: metrics boards understand, budgets and vendors, technical due diligence, crisis leadership, and communicating up without surprises.

docs.title: If you are cramming
docs.text: For a Staff/Principal loop this week: Design docs and the path — Staff interviews increasingly test document craft and level-appropriate framing directly, and the levels page pairs with both. The rest of the section is career infrastructure: read it when you're doing the job, not just interviewing for it.

docs.title: Architecture Reviews
docs.text: Run the arithmetic on a Staff+ calendar: you'll review ten designs for every one you author. That ratio makes review the mechanism by which your judgment scales — the place where one person's scar tissue becomes forty teams' prevented incidents. It's also a social craft with real failure modes: the review that rubber-stamps, the review that bikesheds, the reviewer who wins arguments and loses colleagues. This page is both halves — the technical toolkit and the human one.

docs.title: The reviewer's toolkit, consolidated
docs.text:
This site has been arming you for reviews all along; here is the kit assembled. Five questions find most problems in most designs:

  1. "What must be true for this to work?" — surfaces the unstated assumptions: the cache hit rate the capacity plan silently requires, the clock synchronization the dedup logic leans on, the vendor rate limit nobody checked.
  2. "What breaks first at 10×?" — the bottleneck sequence, demanded rather than performed. Designs that can't answer haven't met their own load.
  3. "What's the blast radius when — not if — it fails?" — the four questions per component, correlated-failure hunting ("what do the replicas share?"), and the metastability probe: "what sustains overload after the trigger clears?"
  4. "Why not the boring alternative?" — the innovation-token check. If the doc's alternatives section can't make Postgres-and-a-queue lose fairly, the proposal is fashion.
  5. "Who operates this at 3 a.m.?" — observability, runbooks, on-call ownership, and the org question hiding inside: does the proposed boundary match a team that can actually carry the pager?

Behind those five, the deeper sweeps as time allows: the coordination inventory ("does this really need agreement?"), the cost paragraph's presence, the assume-breach walk, the migration path ("how do we get there from here — and back?"), and the one-way-door check (is the shard key / API shape / datastore choice getting ceremony proportional to its irreversibility?).

docs.title: Feedback that lands
docs.text:
The technical finding is worthless if the delivery makes the author defensive. The mechanics that keep reviews productive:

  • Critique the design, never the designer. "This design loses writes during failover" opens an investigation; "you didn't think about failover" opens a fight.
  • Ask real questions before making statements. "What happens to in-flight requests during the resharding?" is Socratic and genuinely humble — sometimes there's an answer you missed, and the question finds it either way. (Fake-Socratic gotcha questions, where you know and they know you know, read as theater. Ask because you want the answer.)
  • Rank your objections, out loud. "Two blocking concerns, three preferences — the blockers are the failover story and the cost model; everything else, your call." Unranked feedback forces authors to treat nitpicks and showstoppers identically, which is how twenty-comment reviews produce zero changes. The blocking/non-blocking distinction is the single highest-value habit in review culture.
  • Bring the smaller version, not just the no. A review that kills a design owes the author the salvage: "the full event-sourcing rebuild doesn't clear the bar, but the CDC-plus-projections 80% version gets your actual requirement for a tenth the cost." Killing projects kindly is a Staff skill; the estimation kill ("that's 3 PB/year before we start") remains the gentlest weapon, because arithmetic isn't personal.
  • State confidence and what would change your mind. "Strong opinions, loosely held" operationalized: "I'm 80% sure the fan-out breaks at celebrity scale — a load test on the follower distribution would settle it." That sentence invites evidence instead of escalation, and it models calibration for the room.
  • Disagree and commit — in that order, explicitly. When you lose the argument: dissent on the record (in the ADR), then genuinely commit — no relitigating in hallways, no told-you-so incident laps. The record protects you; the commitment protects the org.

And receiving review, since Staff+ authors are also the most-reviewed: steel-man the objection before answering it, thank the finder of the genuinely bad news (they just saved you a postmortem), and treat "I don't understand this section" as a writing bug, not a reader bug.

docs.title: Review as an institution
docs.text:
Individual reviews are judgment; the review system is governance, and someone at Staff+ has to own its health:

  • Match ceremony to blast radius (the two-way-door rule, applied): lightweight async review for reversible choices; full-panel review for one-way doors. An org that reviews everything heavily reviews nothing well.
  • The production-readiness review (PRR) is ops' structured contribution — a launch-gate checklist (SLOs defined? runbooks? capacity model? rollback rehearsed? security walk done?) that converts this site's callouts into a gate. Checklists catch the forgettable; reviewers save their attention for the novel.
  • Watch the reviewer bottleneck. When every design queues behind three trusted reviewers, review became the org's serial fraction. The fix is deliberate: delegate reviews with your rubric (this page is a training doc — use it), pair juniors into reviews as apprenticeship, and reserve the scarce senior attention for the one-way doors.
  • Mind the standards/judgment boundary. Consistency questions ("we use Postgres unless waived") belong in written standards enforced by paved roads, not relitigated per review; judgment questions (is this the right boundary?) deserve humans. Reviews that spend their minutes on the former starve the latter.

DevOps lens

Operational experience is the single scarcest input in most review rooms — deploy it deliberately: you are the person who asks about connection-pool sizing under the failover, the reconnect stampede after the deploy, the conntrack table on the NAT path, and who carries the pager — questions whiteboard-native reviewers structurally miss. Two ops-specific review instruments: demand the deploy story (a design without its rollout, rollback, and migration choreography is a partial submission — send it back the way an editor returns a draft), and read the on-call implications as a first-class cost ("this adds a fourth stateful system to a team already at 2 pages/shift" is a blocking concern, not a lifestyle complaint). The PRR checklist above is yours to institutionalize — nobody else in the building will.

Staff+ altitude

Markers: (1) Review is your teaching medium — the goal isn't catching this design's bugs but upgrading this team's next design; explain the principle behind the finding (the four questions, not just today's instance) and the review compounds. (2) Track your own calibration — revisit your blocking objections quarterly: which materialized? A reviewer who blocks often and is rarely vindicated is spending credibility they'll need; the honest ledger keeps you sharp and keeps your "blocking" label meaningful. (3) The quarterly read-across — reviews are sensor data: the same gap appearing in five teams' designs (nobody writes cost sections; everyone hand-rolls retries) is a platform gap or strategy gap, not five coincidences. Reviews feed strategy the way postmortems feed reliability. (4) Protect dissent structurally — the review culture where juniors safely question Staff designs is the one that catches Staff mistakes; ask "what's the strongest case against my own position?" out loud, and mean it.

In the interview

The design-review interview — critique a flawed architecture — is now standard in Staff+ loops, and it's this page performed under a clock. The winning structure: inventory before attacking (two minutes absorbing: restate the design's goal and shape — evaluators score comprehension first), rank the top three risks ("the blocking concerns are the split-brain window in this failover, the hot-partition exposure of this key choice, and the missing degraded mode; I'd also note, non-blocking, ..."), probe with questions where the doc is silent rather than assuming the worst, and end with the salvage — what you'd keep, the smaller version that works. The differentiator matching real reviews: ranked findings with confidence attached, versus the candidate who fires twenty equal-weight objections. And bring one review story to behavioral rounds: the design you blocked (and what happened), or better — the one you un-blocked by finding the smaller version.

Next: Technology strategy — the layer above reviews: choosing the defaults that make a thousand designs converge on their own.

docs.title: Design Docs, RFCs & ADRs
docs.text: Here is the transition nobody announces: at Staff+, your primary output stops being code and becomes documents that make groups of people converge. A design doc is not paperwork about the work — at this level it is the work: one document read by thirty engineers, three teams, and two skeptical directors does what no amount of hallway persuasion can, and it keeps doing it while you sleep. Writing them well is the single highest-leverage skill on the Staff+ ladder, and it's a craft with learnable mechanics.

docs.title: Why writing wins
docs.text: Four properties no meeting has: scale (1 doc : N readers, asynchronously, across time zones); rigor (prose exposes hand-waving that slides survive — "we'll handle failover appropriately" dies on the page); memory (the org can re-read why long after everyone involved has left — see ADRs below); and fairness (the best argument wins on paper more often than the loudest voice wins in a room). Amazon banned slides for prose memos on exactly this reasoning; you don't need the mandate to adopt the practice.

docs.title: Anatomy of a design doc
docs.text:
The structure below is the industry's convergent form — and every section maps to a discipline this site already taught:

  1. Context & problem — what hurts, for whom, with numbers (estimation opens documents just like it opens interviews). One paragraph a new reader can follow.
  2. Goals / non-goals — the scope contract (the core-three move, written down). Non-goals are the load-bearing half: they're where you pre-empt the scope creep that kills reviews.
  3. Proposed design — the skeleton: stores, flows, boundaries; diagrams with labeled arrows; the sync/async line drawn. Depth proportional to risk — deep on the novel parts, one line on the boring ones.
  4. Alternatives considered — the section experienced reviewers read first, and the honesty test of the whole document: a doc with weak alternatives is an advertisement, not an analysis. Each alternative gets its genuine best case and the specific reason it loses here (the trade-off template, in prose). Always include the boring option and "do nothing."
  5. Failure modes & risks — the four questions, answered before reviewers ask them; blast radius; the degraded modes.
  6. Rollout & migration — how we get there from here, reversibly (expand-contract, flags, waves); the design that can't be shipped incrementally isn't finished.
  7. Cost — the costed paragraph: order-of-magnitude infra + engineering time. Its absence is the most common Staff-doc gap.
  8. Open questions & decision requested — what you actually need from readers. A doc that doesn't name its decision gets discussion instead of decisions.

Ceremony scales with reversibility. A two-way-door decision (easily reversed) deserves a one-pager and a fast yes; a one-way door (shard keys, public APIs, datastore choices, org boundaries) deserves the full form and real review. Applying heavyweight process to reversible choices is how organizations calcify; applying lightweight process to irreversible ones is how they end up on the migration treadmill.

docs.title: Writing craft: the moves that separate good docs
docs.text:
• Write the reader's objections first. Draft, then simulate your three toughest reviewers and answer them in the text. A pre-empted objection is a review comment that never happens.
  • Numbers over adjectives. "Significant load" is an argument; "40k QPS peak, [do the math]" is a fact. Every "large/slow/expensive" in a draft is a place a number should be.
  • BLUF — bottom line up front. Recommendation in the first five lines. Readers who trust you stop there; skeptics read on with the conclusion framing the evidence. Suspense is for novels.
  • One idea per document. The doc proposing a new queue and a reorg and a datastore migration gets none of them approved. Split it.
  • Make it skimmable — headers that carry the argument by themselves, bold key sentences, tables for enumerable things (the same rules as this site, because reading is reading).

docs.title: The RFC process: socialize, then broadcast
docs.text:
The document is half the craft; the process is the other half:

  1. Draft privately, review early. Show a rough draft to 2–3 people whose objections are most dangerous — before wide circulation. Never surprise a stakeholder in public: the co-worker who first sees your proposal-touching-their-system in a 40-person channel is now an opponent regardless of the proposal's merits. Pre-socialization converts your sharpest critics into co-authors (credit them; it's cheap and true).
  2. Circulate with a clock. Comment period with an explicit end ("decision Friday"), a named decision-maker, and the decision requested stated up front. RFCs without deadlines become permanent conversations.
  3. Run the review meeting for the open questions only. Pre-read culture (or Amazon-style silent reading at the start); the author steers discussion away from what's already settled in the text and toward the genuinely open. The meeting's output is a decision, minuted in the doc.
  4. Record the outcome — including the dissent. "Decided: option B. Objections noted: X's concern about cross-region latency — accepted risk, revisit at 10× traffic." Dissent-on-the-record is what makes disagree and commit legitimate rather than steamrolling.

docs.title: ADRs: institutional memory as a practice
docs.text:
An Architecture Decision Record is the design doc's minimalist sibling: half a page per significant decision — context, decision, consequences — numbered and versioned in the repo next to the code. The org failure it prevents is one you've met: "why is it like this?" — "nobody knows, it predates everyone" — which is how load-bearing accidents become permanent. ADRs make Chesterton's fence inspectable: future engineers (and future you) can distinguish "deliberate trade-off, still valid" from "2019 expedient, safe to remove." The discipline that makes them work: written at decision time (not archaeologically), immutable once accepted (superseded by new ADRs, never edited — the append-only ledger instinct, applied to decisions), and linked from the code they govern.

DevOps lens

Ops already runs on this craft under other names — recognize the transfer and you're halfway trained: a postmortem is a design doc about the past (context, timeline, contributing factors, decisions requested — the blameless discipline is the alternatives-considered honesty); a runbook is a design doc for 3 a.m. (BLUF taken literally: mitigation first, diagnosis later); a production-readiness review is an RFC with a checklist. The ops-specific edge in design docs: your failure-modes and rollout sections will be the best in the room, because you've lived what happens when they're missing. The ops-specific gap: cost and organizational consequences sections — lean on the cost page and org design deliberately.

Staff+ altitude

Markers: (1) Templates are a paved road — a good org-wide design-doc template (with the failure-modes, cost, and rollout sections pre-printed as headings) raises every team's floor; shipping one is a classic Staff move (the paved-road argument, applied to thinking). (2) Docs have SLOs too — review turnaround norms ("48h for one-pagers, one week for RFCs") prevent the graveyard-of-unreviewed-docs failure mode that teaches engineers to stop writing. (3) The decision log is a strategy input — a quarterly read-across of ADRs reveals the org's actual technical direction (as opposed to the stated one) the same way postmortem read-acrosses reveal systemic fragility; both feed strategy. (4) Kill the meeting-first culture by substitution, not decree — every time a decision meeting looms, pre-empt it with a one-pager and watch the meeting shrink to twenty minutes of open questions. Demonstrated, the practice spreads itself.

In the interview

Staff+ loops test this directly and indirectly. Directly: "walk me through a design doc you wrote" — have one rehearsed (sanitized), and walk it as this page's anatomy: the problem's numbers, the non-goals, the alternative you rejected and why it was genuinely tempting, the rollout, what you'd change now. The alternatives discussion is where the evaluation happens — it's trade-off maturity with receipts. Indirectly: some companies run written exercises (a one-hour mini-RFC) — the anatomy above is your template, BLUF discipline is your time manager. And in every verbal design interview, the doc mindset shows up as structure: goals/non-goals stated, alternatives named before the interviewer asks, the decision-requested habit becoming "here's my recommendation and the trade I'm accepting." Interviewers describe that candidate as "already operating at the level" — which is, recall, how the level is actually granted.

Next: Architecture reviews — the other side of the table, where you'll spend ten hours for every one you spend authoring.

docs.title: Executive Craft
docs.text: The CTO's actual job is holding technology and business in productive tension — translating in both directions without losing fidelity in either. Everything below is translation craft: metrics the board can act on, budgets that survive the CFO, vendors negotiated from evidence, due diligence that finds the truth in two weeks, and the communication discipline that underwrites all of it — never bring a surprise, never bring a mystery without a plan. This is the layer where the whole site becomes business language.

docs.title: Metrics that matter upward
docs.text:
Boards and executive peers can't act on p99s; they can act on trends, risks, and unit economics. The one-page engineering health report that works:

  • Delivery: the DORA four — deploy frequency, lead time, change-failure rate, MTTR — as trends, because they're architecture outputs, and their direction says whether the machine is improving.
  • Reliability: SLO attainment on the 3–5 journeys the business dies without, plus incident count/severity trend. Not uptime theater — budget language: "we spent 60% of checkout's error budget this quarter; here's what bought it."
  • Economics: unit cost trends (cost per order/user/GB) and gross-margin impact — the translation of every infrastructure decision into the language the room already speaks.
  • Risk posture: time-to-patch criticals, audit findings open/closed, DR test recency, key-person exposure. Risk reported routinely is governance; risk reported only in crises is a career event.
  • Anti-metrics, refused out loud: lines of code, story points, utilization. When asked for them, offer the DORA set instead and explain why — teaching the board to read engineering is part of the job.

docs.title: Budgets: headcount, run-vs-grow, and the CFO conversation
docs.text: Engineering budgets are ~70–80% people, so the budget conversation is really the headcount-allocation conversation: what fraction runs the business (keep-the-lights-on, platform, compliance) versus grows it (product bets)? Making that split explicit — "we're 35% run today; the platform investment takes it to 30% by year-end, releasing two teams' worth of capacity" — converts an opaque cost center into a managed portfolio. Cloud spend rides the same discipline: unit economics, commitment curves, and the levers ranked, presented as forecast plus options ("spend lands at $X on trend; levers A/B/C trade savings against velocity thus"). The relationship rule with finance: surprises are the only unforgivable sin — a CFO who trusts your forecasts funds your bets; one burned by month-end archaeology audits them.

docs.title: Vendor strategy: negotiate from telemetry
docs.text: Enterprise agreements are won by whoever has better data about your usage — so be that party: usage curves and growth forecasts turn list-price conversations into commitment-curve math. The contract traps to read for personally: egress and exit costs (data gravity is the real lock-in — price the leaving, not just the staying), auto-renew windows, seat minimums that outlive reorgs, and support tiers that gate the SLA you assumed. Portfolio-level discipline: know your tier-0 vendors (the ones whose outage is your outage — DNS, CDN, cloud, identity) and hold a rehearsed posture per: multi-vendor where the abstraction is cheap, documented-exit where it isn't, and accepted-dependency-with-eyes-open where the integration value wins — written down, like every risk acceptance.

docs.title: Technical due diligence: truth in two weeks
docs.text:
Whether acquiring, being acquired, or being invested in, tech DD is the same exam — and it's mostly about people and honesty, not architecture aesthetics:

  • The team: bus factor on critical systems (who can safely change the ledger?), key-person risk, hero culture (one name in every incident is fragility wearing a cape), attrition in the git history.
  • Architecture honesty: ask for the diagram, then trace one real request with an engineer — the gap between diagram and reality is the debt measure. "What breaks at 10× users?" separates teams that know their system from teams that hope.
  • Operational maturity: postmortems exist and are blameless? Deploys automated and frequent? Restore actually tested? Who has prod access? These four questions predict integration cost better than any code review.
  • The registers: tech debt, risk, dependency/licensing (the GPL surprise genre), security posture. An org with honest registers is buyable; an org with none is an archaeology project priced as a company.
  • Red flags that reprice deals: everything-depends-on-the-founder's-laptop, no staging environment, compliance claims without evidence, and — subtle but damning — no one can say why past decisions were made (the ADR argument, now with a valuation attached).

docs.title: Risk and governance
docs.text: The executive risk instrument is the register: each risk with likelihood × impact, a named owner, the mitigation state, and a review cadence — the SLO discipline applied to uncertainty. The CTO-specific craft is framing: compliance as market access (SOC 2 is a sales unlock, not a tax — the platform you built makes the audit a report), security investment sized to the threat model (controls scale to attacker budget, argued with the same actuarial honesty as chaos spend), and accepted risks stated in writing — "we accept single-region operation until Series C; here's the exposure math" is governance; the same fact undocumented is negligence with better vibes.

docs.title: Crisis leadership: the seat above the incident
docs.text: In a SEV1, the executive's job is everything except the keyboard: decision rights (pre-delegated: the IC runs the response, full stop — an exec who grabs the keyboard beheads the command structure), air cover (absorbing the board/customer pressure so responders work), the external cadence (communicate on rhythm, not on progress — customers, execs, and if needed press, each with calibrated honesty), and the aftermath posture: blameless upward too — the CTO who ships the systemic-fix narrative ("here's the class of failure and the investment that removes it") converts the worst week into the reliability budget's best argument. Crises are also when the org learns what you're made of; the calm is the job.

docs.title: Communicating up: the memo discipline
docs.text:
The mechanics that keep executive trust compounding: BLUF always (bottom line up front — an exec summary a board member can act on without reading further); three options, one recommendation (never a mystery without a plan, never a single option disguised as a choice); pre-reads over presentations (the design-doc culture, promoted to the boardroom); and trend + risk + ask as the standing update format — what's moving, what could hurt us, what I need from you. The compounding asset is calibration: the executive whose "this worries me" has always been worth worrying about gets believed at the moment it matters most.

DevOps lens

An operations background is quietly a CTO asset — the translations are already native: cost fluency (the bill as architecture) is the CFO bridge; risk realism (you've seen the failure modes, so your risk register is grounded, not theoretical); crisis calm (incident command is exec-crisis training with receipts); and evidence habits (SLOs, error budgets, postmortems) are governance instincts most executives have to be taught. The gap to close deliberately: revenue-side fluency — how the product makes money, what customers churn over, what sales promises — because a CTO who only speaks cost-and-risk becomes the department of no. Spend real time with sales calls and churn analyses; the strategy kernel needs a business diagnosis, not just an infrastructure one.

Staff+ altitude

This page is also the Staff/Principal job description one level early — the right-hand archetype (the path) does exactly this work without the title: drafting the board slide's technical section, running the DD checklist on the acquisition target, writing the memo that becomes the strategy. Three ways to practice from the current seat: (1) write the quarterly engineering-health one-pager for your area (DORA + SLO + unit cost + risks) whether anyone asked or not — it will get forwarded upward, with your name on it; (2) sit in on a vendor negotiation or audit — the mechanics are learnable only by exposure; (3) translate one incident into business language for a non-technical audience and watch which framings land. The Staff engineer who can already speak this dialect is the one in the room when the CTO conversation happens.

In the interview

CTO/VP conversations are narrative screens with business-fluency checks woven in: "walk me through a technology strategy you set" (the kernel, with outcomes); "tell me about your hardest incident" — told from the command seat: decision rights, comms cadence, the systemic fix, the customer outcome; "how would you assess our platform in your first 90 days?" — the DD checklist above, framed as listen-first ("team, honesty gap, operational maturity, registers — and I'd ship one visible fix in the first month"); and the economics screen, often disguised as small talk: "what's your take on our infra spend?" wants unit economics instinct, not a number. Preparation is the same three written stories plus one addition — a point of view on the business: a CTO candidate without an opinion on how the company should win is interviewing for the wrong room. And at every level below CTO, this material compounds too: the Staff candidate who can say "here's how I'd explain this migration to the board" has just answered a question nobody else in the loop can.

Section complete. The craft around the systems — now go back and notice how every Staff+ callout in the other twelve sections was quietly teaching this all along.

docs.title: Engineering Org Design
docs.text: Conway's law — systems mirror the communication structures of the organizations that build them — is usually quoted as a warning. At Staff+ it's a tool: since you ship your org chart regardless, design the org chart with the same rigor you'd design the system, because it is the system's first architecture diagram. This page treats teams the way the rest of the site treats services: boundaries, interfaces, capacity limits, and failure modes — because the USL applies to humans with brutal fidelity, and coordination cost is the org's quadratic term.

docs.title: Conway's law, wielded
docs.text:
The passive version of Conway's law produces the classic pathology: an org of four teams builds a system with four components whether or not four is the right number, and every team boundary becomes a system boundary complete with API friction, latency hops, and political ownership disputes. The active version — the inverse Conway maneuver — runs the causality deliberately: choose the architecture you want, then shape teams to match it, and let the org's natural mirroring work for you instead of against you.

The corollary that should be said in every architecture review: every service boundary proposal is a team boundary proposal. Microservices without matching team ownership is a distributed monolith with extra steps — you pay the network tax and the coordination tax without buying the independent-deployment benefit, because five teams still have to synchronize every release. The system decomposition and the org decomposition are one decision wearing two diagrams; drawing them separately is how both end up wrong.

docs.title: Team topologies: the four shapes
docs.text:
Skelton and Pais's Team Topologies gave the industry its working vocabulary — four team types, and the discipline is that there are only four:

  • Stream-aligned — owns a product/user-journey slice end to end; the default type, the ones that ship value directly. Everything else exists to make these faster.
  • Platform — builds the paved roads: the deploy pipeline, the observability stack, the golden-path templates. Product mindset mandatory (adoption is the metric); its failure modes are the ivory tower (built without customers, adopted by nobody) and ticket-ops (a human queue with no leverage — the platform is supposed to be self-serve).
  • Enabling — teaches and moves on: embeds with a team to install a capability (SRE practices, security habits), then leaves. The leaving is definitional — an enabling team that stays is a dependency, not an enabler.
  • Complicated-subsystem — owns the genuinely specialist piece (the ML ranking core, the ledger, the codec pipeline) where deep expertise beats broad ownership.

And three interaction modes: collaboration (working together — expensive, high-bandwidth, temporary by design), X-as-a-service (consuming through an interface — cheap, scalable), and facilitating (the enabling mode). The trap the vocabulary exists to catch: everything drifting into collaboration — every team pairing with every team on everything, which is coordination soup: the all-to-all topology whose cost grows quadratically. Healthy orgs push interactions toward X-as-a-service over time — which is precisely what good platform boundaries and team APIs enable.

docs.title: Cognitive load: the real sizing metric
docs.text:
Teams aren't sized by headcount arithmetic; they're sized by what fits in the team's collective head. A team owning three services it understands deeply outperforms one owning nine it understands shallowly — shallow ownership is where gray failures live undetected, where on-call becomes roulette, and where "we don't touch that one" systems accumulate. The overload symptoms are legible if you look: onboarding measured in quarters, pager fatigue, rising MTTR on systems the team nominally owns, and the tell-tale "we'd need to ask the person who wrote it" (a bus-factor alarm ringing).

The design responses: shrink the surface (hand a service to a team with headroom — an org migration, run with the migration playbook), or shrink the load per service — which is exactly what platforms are for: every concern the paved road absorbs (resilience defaults, pipeline templates, identity) is cognitive load deleted from every stream-aligned team at once. Platform investment is cognitive-load arbitrage.

docs.title: Ownership and the on-call economics
docs.text:
You build it, you run it is the DevOps thesis applied to org design: the team that carries the pager for its own code closes the feedback loop that makes systems operable — operability instincts grow precisely where the pain lands. The main variants: full ownership (build+run, the default to aim for), SRE-partnered (a reliability team engages with the product team still owning the service — Google's model, viable at scale, dangerous when it decays into "throw it over the wall"), and platform-mediated (the platform absorbs the infrastructure pager so product teams carry only their application's).

The arithmetic nobody escapes: a humane on-call rotation needs ~6–8 people (one week in six minimum; below that you're burning humans — sustained pages degrade judgment and then retention). That number is a hard org-design constraint: a three-person team cannot own a tier-0 service, no matter how skilled — the rotation math forbids it. Every proposal that creates a new stateful, paged system implicitly proposes the team to carry it; reviews should read that cost out loud.

docs.title: Team APIs: the org version of service contracts
docs.text:
A team, like a service, needs a published interface: what it owns (services + SLOs), how to request work (and the SLA on responses), its roadmap, its docs, its communication channels, its on-call boundary. Teams with clear APIs get consumed X-as-a-service; teams without them get consumed by meetings — every interaction escalates to synchronous collaboration because there's no contract to rely on. The same additive-evolution discipline applies: changing a team's interface (ownership transfers, intake changes) deserves versioning, notice, and a migration path for its consumers.

And the change process itself: a reorg is a migration of the human system — the same playbook applies (derisk with the hardest case, enable with clarity, finish completely), the same failure mode looms (the 90% reorg, where old and new structures both half-exist, is worse than either — two sources of truth for ownership means none). The cheapest reorg remains the one you avoid: don't reorganize what an interface fix would solve — if two teams fight at a boundary, try redrawing the API before redrawing the org chart.

DevOps lens

On-call is the org's observability: pager load per team is the single most honest org-health metric in existence — it can't be gamed in a slide deck, it locates cognitive overload precisely, and its trend predicts attrition quarters ahead. Read the org through it: a team at 3+ pages/shift is under-platformed, over-scoped, or carrying someone else's tech debt interest; a system nobody wants to inherit in a reorg is telling you its real operability score. Ops also holds the org's coordination ledger: incident retros reveal the true team boundaries — who had to be paged into whose incident, where the runbook said "contact team X and wait" — the postmortem read-across is a Conway diagram of where the org chart and the architecture disagree. Bring that data to org-design conversations and you'll be the only person in the room arguing from evidence.

Staff+ altitude

Markers: (1) Say the quiet part in reviews — "this proposal creates a fourth service for a team of five; that's a topology decision, not just a technical one" — making the org cost of architecture explicit is a Staff move that managers will thank you for. (2) Know the ratios as priors, not laws — platform investment commonly lands at 10–20% of engineering (rising with org size), EM:IC spans of 5–8, and the 6–8 on-call floor; deviations aren't wrong, but they need reasons. (3) Design team seams like shard keys — the same four properties: high cohesion inside, low coordination across, aligned with the change patterns ("what changes together lives together"), and hard to re-cut later — team boundaries are one-way-door-ish, so they get one-way-door ceremony. (4) The inverse Conway is a Staff+ lever precisely because you don't control the org chart — you control the architecture proposals that the org chart will eventually mirror; propose boundaries you'd want teams to have, and say so in the doc.

In the interview

Org questions enter loops at senior-Staff and above — "how would you structure teams for this product?" — and the winning move is treating it as system design, because it is: requirements (product streams, scale, the specialist cores) → boundaries (stream-aligned teams sized by cognitive load, a platform team once the rule of three bites, complicated-subsystem teams only where expertise genuinely concentrates) → interfaces (team APIs, X-as-a-service defaults, where collaboration is temporarily intentional) → failure modes (the coordination hotspot you're watching, the on-call math, how the design evolves at 2× headcount). Name Conway explicitly and interviewers at this level relax — it signals you know the game. For CTO conversations, add the honest constraint: "I'd shape this around the people we actually have, then evolve toward the target — org design with real humans is migration, not greenfield." That sentence separates people who've done it from people who've read about it.

Next: Executive craft — the CTO layer: where technology meets capital, boards, and the art of never bringing a surprise.

docs.title: Technology Strategy
docs.text: Most documents titled "technology strategy" are shopping lists — a parade of initiatives with no connecting logic and nothing declined. Real strategy is the opposite shape: a diagnosis of the situation, a guiding policy that says no to most things, and a small set of coherent actions (Rumelt's kernel, which survives translation into engineering perfectly). Strategy's test is whether it makes future decisions easier: a good one lets a hundred engineers resolve their next hundred choices without asking you. This page is the working kit: the kernel, the frameworks that recur (tokens, radar, build-vs-buy), the platform play, and the org-scale skill that underwrites all of it — migrations.

docs.title: The kernel: diagnosis → policy → actions
docs.text:
Worked, in this site's vocabulary: Diagnosis — "we run nine datastores; each is a permanent line item of backups, expertise, and 3 a.m. pages; operational load is our real velocity limit" (with the incident-tax arithmetic as evidence — diagnoses need numbers or they're vibes). Guiding policy — "consolidate to a blessed few; new stores require a waiver with named access patterns." Coherent actions — the paved-road library, the migration plan off the orphans, the waiver process, the review-gate enforcement.

Notice what the policy does: it declines things. A strategy that permits everything is a mission statement. The other tell of real strategy: it's falsifiable — "we bet consolidation beats best-tool-per-job at our scale" can turn out wrong, and the ADR trail lets you notice.

docs.title: Innovation tokens: spend where you differentiate
docs.text:
Dan McKinley's Choose Boring Technology compressed a career of judgment into one metaphor: an organization gets about three innovation tokens. Every non-boring choice — the exotic database, the young framework, the bespoke orchestrator — spends one, because novel technology means unknown failure modes, thin hiring pools, and operational surface you'll staff forever. Boring ≠ bad: boring means the failure modes are documented — Postgres's sharp edges are known; the six-month-old streaming engine's are yours to discover in production.

The policy that falls out: spend tokens only where the business differentiates. A fintech's token belongs in the ledger, not the message queue; an ML company's in the training platform, not the web framework. And the corollary discipline the site keeps teaching: the boring-technology thesis is a strategy, not a lack of ambition — the KV-store case study's "I'd buy unless exceptional needs" confession is token management out loud.

docs.title: The tech radar: governance with a heartbeat
docs.text: The adopt / trial / assess / hold radar (ThoughtWorks' form) works less as a document than as a ritual: a quarterly forcing function where the org decides together what's blessed, what's on probation, and — the hard quadrant — what's on hold (no new usage; existing usage has a migration destination). The craft points: keep it small (a radar with 200 blips is a phone book); every trial entry names an owner and an exit date (probation without review is adoption by drift); every hold entry names the replacement path (hold without a destination is just guilt). The radar is where review-sensor data becomes policy: five teams hand-rolling the same thing → it goes on the radar as a platform candidate.

docs.title: Build vs. buy vs. adopt: the full frame
docs.text:
The recurring decision, systematized. Four tests in order:

  1. Differentiation — is this capability why customers choose us? No → strong buy/adopt prior. (The notification system's "buy the engine, own the contract" is the pattern: differentiate at the interface, commoditize the implementation.)
  2. Total cost, with headcount honesty — the build estimate that omits the forever cost (on-call, upgrades, security patching — typically 2–5× the initial build over five years) isn't an estimate, it's a pitch. The estimation discipline applies to org decisions too.
  3. Exit cost — what does leaving cost in three years? Data gravity, API coupling, format lock-in. Open formats and owned interfaces (the lakehouse position) are exit insurance worth paying small premiums for.
  4. The scale-flip check — buy-then-build is a legitimate sequence, not indecision: the SaaS bill crossing self-host cost is a predictable curve; strategy is naming the flip point in advance ("we revisit at $X/month or Y volume") rather than discovering it in a budget crisis.

docs.title: Platform strategy: leverage as a product
docs.text: Every paved-road argument in this site — the cache client, the resilience defaults, the pipeline templates, the identity scheme — is one strategy expressed repeatedly: encode judgment into defaults, and forty teams inherit it without meetings. The strategic framing: a platform is a product whose customers are engineers, and its adoption must be earned, not mandated — voluntary adoption is the quality signal (an internal platform that needs a decree is failing its market test). The sequencing rule: platformize on the rule of three — extract the shared thing after the third team builds it, when the requirements are proven; platform-first speculation builds ivory towers (the org-design page covers the team shape). And measure platforms like products: adoption %, time-to-first-deploy on the paved road, tickets-per-team — not features shipped.

docs.title: Migrations: the org-scale skill
docs.text:
Will Larson's line deserves its fame: "migrations are the only mechanism to effectively manage technical debt as a company grows." Every strategy above — consolidation, radar holds, platform adoption — is executed as a migration, which makes migration competence the difference between strategy and slideware. The playbook:

  1. Derisk first — prove the destination on the hardest representative case, not the easiest (the easy-case pilot that collapses at case #3 is a genre). This is canary thinking applied to strategy.
  2. Enable — make the migration self-serve: tooling, codemods, docs, a paved path so each team's cost is hours, not weeks. The migration team's product is the other teams' ease.
  3. Finish — the actual skill. The last 10% (the weird legacy service, the team with no bandwidth) is where migrations die, and a 90% migration is often negative value: two systems, two on-calls, two mental models, forever. Track completion publicly, keep leadership attention on the tail, and staff the finish like it matters — because the payoff only exists at 100%.

The strategic corollary: never start a migration you can't finish — each abandoned one spends organizational trust that the next one needs. And its cousin for debt at large: manage tech debt as a portfolio (deliberate vs. accidental, prudent vs. reckless) and pay down where interest is highest — the debt in the hot path of change, not the ugly-but-frozen corner nobody touches.

DevOps lens

Ops holds the best strategy inputs in the building — use them as evidence: the operational-load census (pages per team per week, toil percentage, incident-hours by system — the incident tax) is the diagnosis section of most good infrastructure strategies; "the nine datastores cost us 340 engineer-hours last quarter" beats any architectural aesthetics. Count the pagers as the consolidation argument. And own the strategy execution instrument: deprecation is an operational program — usage telemetry per client, brownouts, sunset dates — and the migration playbook's enable step is pure platform-ops craft. The trap to avoid: strategies written purely from ops pain optimize for quiet pagers over product velocity; pair every load-reduction bet with the product-facing win it funds, or the business will (correctly) decline it.

Staff+ altitude

Markers: (1) Write the one-page strategy memo — kernel-shaped (diagnosis with numbers, policy, first three actions), BLUF, circulated to the skeptics before the broadcast (the socialization rule); most orgs have zero written technical strategy, so the first person who writes one credibly becomes the strategist. (2) Co-author with your sharpest opponent — a strategy the influential skeptic helped write needs no selling; a perfect strategy imposed on them needs constant defending. (3) Sequence bets by reversibility — lead with the two-way doors (paved roads, defaults) that build credibility; spend it on the one-way doors (the consolidation migration) once you've banked wins. (4) Review the strategy like an SLO — quarterly: which predictions held? What does the radar's drift say? A strategy nobody re-examines is a decree; the falsifiability is the point.

In the interview

Principal loops ask for strategy directly — "tell me about a technical direction you set" — and the kernel is your narrative spine: diagnosis (with the numbers that made it undeniable) → policy (what you said no to — interviewers listen for the declined things, because that's what distinguishes strategy from planning) → actions → measured outcome, including what you got wrong. Hypothetical versions appear too: "we're spending too much on infrastructure — what do you do?" wants the diagnosis-first instinct (unit economics before line items), not an immediate list of cuts; "would you build or buy X?" wants the four tests run out loud with a named flip point. The token vocabulary lands well used once: "that's an innovation-token question — and this isn't where we differentiate, so it's a buy." Said naturally, it signals an entire worldview; said twice, it's a catchphrase.

Next: Engineering org design — Conway's law wielded on purpose, because you ship your org chart whether you designed it or not.

docs.title: The Path: Senior → Staff → Principal → CTO
docs.text: Titles are lagging indicators. Nobody gets promoted to Staff and then starts doing Staff work — the level is granted to people already operating at it, usually for a year or more. That makes the career question concrete: what is the work of the next level, and how do you start doing it from the seat you're in? This page is the map — what actually changes at each altitude, the archetypes the levels hide, and the moves that make invisible leadership work visible.

docs.title: What actually changes
docs.text:
The levels differ less in difficulty than in medium — each one trades a familiar instrument for a more leveraged, less legible one:

Senior Staff Principal CTO Scope a system a system of systems the org's technical direction business ↔ technology fit Time horizon quarters 1–2 years 2–5 years the company's lifetime Primary artifact code + designs docs, reviews, migrations strategy, standards, bets the org, capital, narrative Impact measured by what you shipped what you enabled what you directed & prevented company outcomes Relationship to code writer reviewer & unblocking writer reader sponsor Ambiguity handled "build this well" "figure out what to build" "figure out what matters" "figure out what business we're in, technically"

Two consequences worth internalizing early. First: the skills are cumulative, not substitutive — a Principal who can't drop to storage-engine depth on demand is a strategist nobody trusts; the altitude is earned by the floor (the levels page made this point for interviews; it's truer for the job). Second: legibility drops as leverage rises — everyone can see your shipped feature; almost nobody sees the outage that didn't happen because your design review caught the split-brain. Managing that visibility gap is a real skill, below.

docs.title: The Staff archetypes
docs.text:
"Staff engineer" hides four different jobs (Will Larson's taxonomy, which the industry adopted because it's true):

  • Tech lead — runs a team's technical direction alongside an EM; the most common path, the most people-facing.
  • Architect — owns a critical area's direction (data platform, API surface, infrastructure) across teams; lives in docs and reviews.
  • Solver — parachuted into the hardest, vaguest problems; deep work, low meeting load, high trust required.
  • Right hand — extends an executive's bandwidth; part strategist, part diplomat, part firefighter.

For a DevOps background, the natural on-ramps are architect (platform/infrastructure direction — the paved-road estate needs an owner and you already think in it) and solver (production's hardest problems have always been yours). Know which game you're playing: the archetypes are evaluated differently, and drifting between them unconsciously reads as unfocused.

docs.title: Operating at the next level before the title
docs.text:
The mechanism of promotion at these levels is demonstrated altitude — and the demonstrations are specific:

  • Write the doc nobody asked for. The design doc or strategy memo that frames a decision the org is circling — clean diagnosis, honest alternatives, a recommendation — is the single most visible unit of Staff work. One good doc changes how the room sees you.
  • Adopt an orphaned problem. Every org has a critical, unowned mess (the flaky deploy pipeline, the unowned cost estate, the alert fatigue everyone endures). Owning one end-to-end — diagnosis, plan, migration, finish — is a Staff project regardless of your title.
  • Make your review presence felt. Show up in design reviews with the questions that change designs; it's the highest-frequency stage on which judgment is visible.
  • Trade glue work for named glue work. Coordination, onboarding, unblocking — essential, invisible, and career-dangerous if unnamed. The fix isn't refusing it; it's converting it into artifacts (the onboarding guide, the runbook standard, the process RFC) that carry your name and outlive the moment.
  • Get a sponsor, not just a mentor. Mentors advise you; sponsors spend their political capital on you — putting your name up in calibration, handing you the visible problem. Sponsors are earned by making them look good: deliver on the scoped thing, then ask for the bigger one.

And the disciplines of staying effective once there: guard maker time (Staff+ calendars fragment; the work that got you here still needs contiguous hours), write to think (your conclusions now travel without you — the doc craft is self-defense), and stay technical on purpose (an hour a week in the code you sponsor; the drills exist for exactly this maintenance).

docs.title: The CTO reality check
docs.text:
"CTO" is three jobs sharing a business card, and conflating them wrecks career plans:

  • Startup CTO (founding/≤30 eng) — the best engineer who also hires: 70% building, 30% recruiting and de-risking. Closest to Staff-with-equity.
  • Scale-up CTO (30–300 eng) — the transition where the job inverts: org design, strategy, hiring machinery, and the first real executive translation duties. Most CTO failures happen here — the skills that built the product don't run the org.
  • Enterprise CTO — portfolio and governance: capital allocation across technology bets, risk, M&A due diligence, external narrative. Often paired with a VP Eng who runs delivery.

The honest sequencing advice: the Staff→Principal path and the management path both lead here, but the CTO seat demands the union — technical credibility (this site's twelve sections) plus the org/capital/narrative craft (the rest of this one). Pick up the second skill set while your first is still sharp.

DevOps lens

Your background maps unusually well onto this ladder — with one warning. The assets: operational judgment is the scarcest Staff+ input (you've seen systems fail, so your reviews and strategies are grounded in failure reality rather than whiteboard optimism); cost fluency is executive language already; incident command is leadership-under-pressure with receipts (the IC bench is where many Staff engineers are first seen). The warning: ops careers can rail into the solver-forever trap — always the firefighter, never the strategist — because firefighting is visible and strategy is slow. The escape is deliberate: after each fire, write the systemic-fix memo; convert incident authority into architectural authority, one document at a time.

Staff+ altitude

The meta-skill of the whole ladder is choosing what to work on — at Senior, work finds you; at Staff+, an infinite queue of plausible work exists and the level is expressed in which 5% you touch. The filter that scales: leverage (does this change what a hundred engineers do?), irreversibility (one-way doors get your attention; two-way doors get delegated — the review lens), and counterfactual (would this happen without you? then let it). Corollary: saying no is the job — every yes at this level is a strategy statement, and a Staff engineer who can't decline visible-but-low-leverage work becomes a very senior task rabbit. The polite no that offers the smaller version or the better owner is a skill worth scripting.

In the interview

Level-specific loops test level-specific media: Staff loops add design-review interviews (critique a flawed design — the toolkit), document exercises ("walk me through a design doc you wrote" — have one sanitized and rehearsed), and cross-team stories (influence without authority: the time you changed another team's direction without escalating).
