Rules that separate real ones from dashboard decoration: measure where the user is (at the LB or client, not inside the service — a service that's "up" behind a dead LB is down; the differential-observability lesson); good/total ratios, not averages (averages lie; "99% of requests under 300 ms" is a percentile statement with a denominator); per user-journey, not per microservice ("checkout completes" spans eleven services — users experience journeys, and availability composes multiplicatively across your internal hops whether you measure it or not); and few (3–5 journeys; twenty SLOs is zero SLOs with extra dashboards).

docs.title: The error budget: the number that changes behavior
docs.text:
An SLO of 99.9% is, flipped over, permission to fail 0.1% of the time — the error budget: ~43 minutes of full downtime (or 43,000 bad requests per 43M) per 30 days. That flip is the whole invention, because a budget is spendable:

  • Budget healthy → ship faster, run riskier experiments, take the aggressive migration. The budget is fuel for velocity, not a safety margin to hoard — chronic under-spend means you're over-investing in reliability nobody asked for (each nine costs 10×, and the money came from somewhere).
  • Budget exhausted → the policy triggers: feature freezes behind reliability work, rollout waves slow, risk review tightens. Written in advance and agreed with product — that pre-agreement is the entire trick: the freeze isn't engineering vetoing product in a heated meeting; it's both sides honoring a treaty they signed in peacetime.

Burn rate is the operational derivative: budget consumed ÷ budget accrued. Burn 1× = exactly on target; burn 14.4× = the 30-day budget gone in 50 hours. And it yields the best alerting math in the industry — multi-window burn-rate alerts: page on fast burn (14.4× over 1 h — a real fire, worth waking someone), ticket on slow burn (3× over 6 h — a smolder, worth tomorrow morning), ignore blips that self-correct. This one construction solves alert fatigue and slow-leak blindness simultaneously — a single bad deploy that 500s 0.05% of requests forever never trips a threshold alert, but it burns budget arithmetic visibly. If you internalize one formula from this section, make it this one.

docs.title: Choosing targets honestly
docs.text:
The right SLO is as low as users tolerate, not as high as engineering can boast: derive from user experience (what staleness/latency/failure do users notice — the staleness-budget conversation, formalized), from business tiering (payments ≠ recommendations — uniform nines are uniform waste), and from measured current performance (an SLO wildly above reality is fiction; wildly below is a free pass — start near reality, tighten deliberately). Remember the dependency arithmetic: your SLO is capped by your critical dependencies' — a 99.99% target atop a chain of 99.9% services is a wish, and making that visible is half of an SLO program's value (soft-dependency design is how you escape the multiplication).

DevOps lens

Implementation truths: the SLI pipeline is production code — computed from LB logs or metrics with histogram buckets placed at the SLO threshold (or your compliance number is interpolation noise); versioned, tested, and owned, because a broken SLI silently corrupts every decision downstream. Dashboards show budget, not just rate — "62% of budget remaining, burn 0.8×, 19 days left in window" is a sentence an engineering director acts on; a latency graph is not. The budget attribution habit: tag burn by cause (deploy-correlated, dependency, capacity, unknown) and the quarterly review writes itself — "70% of burn was change-induced" is the progressive-delivery business case with your own data. And watch the window mechanics: rolling windows heal gradually (a bad day haunts you for 30 days — that's the point); calendar windows reset cliff-edge (and invite month-end risk gaming — rolling is usually the honest choice).

Staff+ altitude

Markers: (1) The SLO is a treaty, and you're its diplomat — the artifact that matters is the signed budget policy (exhaustion consequences, pre-agreed with product leadership); an SLO without an enforced policy is a dashboard with ambitions. (2) SLOs shape architecture money — "we need 99.95 on checkout, which caps us at N serial hard dependencies; everything else goes soft with fallbacks" is the sentence that turns an SLO into a design constraint; and conversely, "this tier only needs 99.5 — delete the multi-region line item" is the cost conversation SLOs exist to enable. (3) Guard against SLO theater — the smells: SLOs nobody can recite, budgets that never trigger policy, SLIs measured where convenient rather than where users are, and twenty objectives per service; the fix is ruthless pruning to the 3–5 journeys the business actually dies without. (4) Error budgets generalize — cost budgets, flake budgets in CI, latency budgets: the pattern (target → budget → burn alerting → pre-agreed policy) is one of the most reusable governance machines in engineering; recognizing it as a pattern is the Staff-level move.

In the interview

Fluency here is disproportionately valuable because the vocabulary is common and the understanding is rare. The set piece: "SLI: good-over-total, measured at the user's edge — say, checkout requests completing under 500 ms. SLO: 99.9% rolling 30 days, which is a 43-minute monthly error budget. Alerting is burn-rate, multi-window: page at 14.4× over an hour, ticket at 3× over six — no threshold noise, no slow-leak blindness. Budget exhausted triggers the pre-agreed policy: freeze risky rollouts, spend the sprint on reliability." Four sentences, complete system, arithmetic included. Probes: "why not just alert on error rate?" (thresholds miss slow burns and page on self-healing blips — burn rate scales urgency to budget impact); "who sets the SLO?" (product + engineering jointly — it's a user-tolerance number, not an engineering pride number); "what if a dependency can't support your SLO?" (the multiplication — make it soft with fallback, or renegotiate the target; naming the cap is the senior move); "99.9 or 99.99?" (the 10×-cost-per-nine answer, ending with "which journeys actually need it?").

Next: Alerting design — the discipline of waking humans correctly.

docs.title: Distributed Tracing
docs.text: A request enters your system and touches eleven services, three caches, two queues, and a database. It took 2.3 seconds. Where did the time go? Metrics can't say — aggregation destroyed the individual. Logs know, but the answer is scattered across eleven services' streams. Tracing is the pillar built for exactly this question: the request's causal story, reassembled — and in a fan-out architecture it's not a luxury, it's the only way "why was this slow?" has an answer at all.

docs.title: The model: spans, context, and the story they assemble
docs.text:
A trace is one request's tree of spans — each span a timed operation (service call, DB query, cache lookup) with attributes, linked to its parent. The assembly trick is context propagation: a trace ID + parent span ID travel with the request (W3C traceparent header for HTTP/gRPC; message metadata across queues), each hop emitting its span tagged with that context; the backend stitches the tree.

The one paragraph of literacy that finds real bugs: reading a trace is reading a waterfall — sequential spans that could parallelize (the N+1 staircase: 45 sequential DB calls that should be one batch is visible as a staircase), gaps between spans (time nobody claims: queue waits, pool waits, GC — the invisible latency that no downstream dashboard shows), and the long pole in a parallel fan-out (the tail-amplification culprit, named and timestamped). Ten minutes with a waterfall replaces ten hours of cross-team latency archaeology — that's the pillar's entire business case.

The propagation fine print that separates practitioners: context must survive async boundaries — through queues (producer injects context into message headers; consumer continues the trace — or your trace ends at the first queue and the event-driven half of your system is dark), through thread pools and async runtimes (context is thread-local in many stacks; executor wrappers exist because context silently dropping at the first async is the classic instrumentation bug), and through your own batch/saga machinery (span links relate one consumed batch to its hundred source traces). A mesh emits spans for hops, but only the app can propagate context through itself — sidecar tracing without app cooperation yields eleven one-span traces, not one eleven-span trace.

docs.title: Sampling: the economics that make it real
docs.text:
Tracing every request at volume is a second product-sized data stream. Sampling is therefore the design decision:

  • Head-based — decide at the front door (keep 1%), propagate the decision so the whole trace is kept or dropped coherently. Cheap, simple, and statistically fine for latency shapes — but it keeps 1% of everything, meaning it misses most rare events: the one-in-ten-thousand error has a 1% chance of being traced. Your worst requests are almost certainly not in your 1%.
  • Tail-based — buffer spans, decide after the trace completes: keep 100% of errors and slow traces, 0.1% of boring successes. Exactly the right data — priced in infrastructure (every span must reach the sampler and wait; a stateful buffering tier with its own scaling story) and complexity.
  • The pragmatic stack most orgs converge on: head-sample a baseline (1–10%) for unbiased shapes + always-sample on error/slow signals where detectable + exemplars linking metric spikes to whatever traces exist + trace-coherent log sampling (keep all logs for sampled traces) so the pillars stay joined.

Instrumentation itself is a solved-ish problem — OpenTelemetry auto-instrumentation covers the standard libraries (HTTP, gRPC, DB drivers, queue clients) with manual spans reserved for business-critical interior logic; the unsolved part is organizational: every service must propagate, because one non-participating hop cuts every trace that crosses it — which is why propagation lives in the paved-road HTTP/queue clients, not in per-team goodwill.

DevOps lens

Tracing's operational truths: broken propagation is the silent default — the dashboard metric is trace completeness (spans-per-trace distribution by route; a route whose traces suddenly halve their span count just lost a participating service — alert on it, because nobody notices missing spans until the incident needs them); clock skew mangles waterfalls (NTP discipline — spans that "start before their parent" are a clock problem, not a physics discovery); the collector tier is real infrastructure (buffer, batch, shed — under incident load, traces burst exactly like logs; decide shed-priority: errors' spans survive, successes' drop); and traces are the incident tool with the fastest time-to-context: the mature runbook motion is metric alert → exemplar → trace → trace-ID-filtered logs — three clicks, one causal story, versus four dashboards and a guess. Teams that drill that motion during calm (game days) use it reflexively at 3 a.m.

Staff+ altitude

Markers: (1) Propagation as platform law — trace context in every paved-road client (HTTP, gRPC, queue, cron), verified by CI conformance tests, because tracing's value is superlinear in coverage (80% coverage ≈ 30% of the value: the dark hops are always where the mystery lives). (2) Sampling policy as a written economic document — baseline rates, always-keep rules, retention, and the tail-sampling build-vs-buy, revisited with volume; "we trace 1% and keep errors always, costing X, answering Y% of incident questions" is the sentence the platform owes the org. (3) Traces as dependency cartography — the aggregate trace corpus is your real service map (who calls whom, how often, how slowly — the architecture diagram that can't lie); mining it for drift against the intended architecture is a genuinely Staff-grade review input. (4) Span semantics governance — naming and attribute conventions (OTel semantic conventions, enforced) so "database span" means one thing fleet-wide; without it, cross-service analysis is the schema-drift problem with extra steps.

In the interview

Deploy tracing at the moment fan-out appears in your design: "eleven services in this path — so trace context propagates end-to-end, including through the queue via message headers; head-sample 5% baseline, always keep errors and >1 s traces; exemplars link the p99 metric to real waterfalls, and logs are trace-coherent so one ID pulls the whole story." Probes: "how does a trace actually work?" (context propagation + span tree + waterfall — then the async-boundary fine print, which is the depth check); "1% sampling — what do you miss?" (rare errors, almost certainly — hence tail-based or always-on-error; knowing head-sampling's blind spot is the practitioner tell); "the trace ends at the queue — why?" (context not injected into message metadata — and the fix's location: the shared client library, not forty teams' diligence). The elite nugget: the gap in the waterfall — "the time nobody claims is the queue wait or pool wait; traces are the only pillar that shows absence." Interviewers who've debugged production latency will recognize the scar.

Next: SLOs & error budgets — reliability turned into arithmetic, and the treaty it creates between engineering and product.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text:
The lookup layer: a glossary of every term this site leans on (each entry linking to the page that teaches it properly), and a curated resource list — the books, papers, and blogs that earn their reading time, annotated with why and when.

  • Glossary

    Every load-bearing term, one-sentence definitions, linked to the page that teaches it in depth.

  • Resources

    The short list that's actually worth your hours: books, papers, engineering blogs, and talks — annotated.

docs.title: AI Practice Harness
docs.text: The site teaches; the harness makes you perform. ai/grail.py is a CLI that turns Claude models (Opus, Sonnet, or Haiku — selectable per session) into practice partners grounded in this site's own pages — the AI interviews, drills, and grades you using the Grail's rubrics, terminology, and expected answers rather than generic internet advice.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
.venv/bin/pip install anthropic
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...    # console.anthropic.com → API keys

docs.title: The four modes
docs.text:
Mock interviewer — a rigorous Staff-level interviewer runs a realistic session: states the prompt, lets you drive, probes trade-offs, kills nodes, scales your numbers 10×, and never lectures. Say :grade and it breaks character to score you on the five rubric axes with level calibration.

python3 ai/grail.py interview -t chat

Drill sergeant — rapid-fire active recall: one question, your answer, a one-word verdict plus the missing clause, next question. Escalates as you hit; circles back to misses. Pairs with the drills page; run it on -m haiku and volume costs pennies.

python3 ai/grail.py drill -t distributed -m haiku

Design reviewer — write a design (timed, ideally — the framework's 45 minutes), then get Staff-review grading: per-axis scores with quoted evidence, the five follow-ups an interviewer would attack next, and the level call.

python3 ai/grail.py review -t news-feed --file my-design.md

Tutor — targeted questions answered in the site's house style (intuition → mechanics → numbers → trade-offs, failure story attached), always citing which page teaches it fully.

python3 ai/grail.py ask -t kafka "When is Kafka the wrong choice?"

docs.title: Practical notes
docs.text:
• Topics: any section (caching, devops, ...) or page slug (chat, kafka, payments). The harness loads those pages as grounding.
  • Cost: the grounding is prompt-cached, so multi-turn sessions re-read it at ~10% price (watch the cache-hit counter in the per-turn usage line). Interviews on Sonnet cost well under a dollar; Haiku drills cost pennies.
  • --dry-run shows exactly what would be sent (model, files, token estimate) without needing an API key.
  • Full details: ai/README.md in the repo.

The practice loop this enables, end to end: study a page → drill it → rehearse the case study out loud → run a mock against the harness → :grade → study the gaps it names. That loop, repeated, is the whole regimen with the partner problem solved.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: One-sentence definitions for every load-bearing term, each linked to the page that teaches it properly. Use search (S) — this page exists to be landed on.

docs.title: A–C
docs.text: ACID — atomicity, consistency, isolation, durability: the single-node transaction contract. → Transactions Active-active / active-passive — redundancy where all nodes serve vs. a standby awaiting failover. → Reliability, Multi-region Amdahl's law — speedup is capped at 1/serial-fraction, no matter the node count. → Scalability Anti-entropy — background reconciliation (often Merkle-tree diff) that converges replicas. → Replication Anycast — one IP announced from many sites; routing delivers users to the nearest. → DNS At-least-once / at-most-once — delivery contracts: retries-with-duplicates vs. fire-and-forget. → Delivery semantics Backpressure — a slow consumer's slowness propagating upstream visibly instead of pooling invisibly. → Async fundamentals Blast radius — the scope of damage when a component fails; a designable quantity. → Reliability Bloom filter — compact probabilistic "definitely not present" test; false positives, never false negatives. → Storage engines Bulkhead — resource partitioning (pools, quotas) so one dependency's failure can't drown the rest. → Resilience Burn rate — error-budget consumption speed; the basis of modern alerting. → SLOs CAP theorem — during a partition, choose consistency or availability; not a triangle. → CAP & PACELC Cardinality — distinct label/value combinations; the master constraint of telemetry cost. → Observability CDC (change data capture) — tailing a database's log to stream every change elsewhere. → Analytics Cell architecture — partitioning the whole stack into independent failure-contained units. → Reliability Circuit breaker — stop calling a failing dependency; fail fast, probe for recovery. → Resilience Consistent hashing — ring-based key placement where membership change moves only K/N keys. → Partitioning CQRS — separate write model from read projections, each shaped for its job. → Event-driven CRDT — data structure whose replicas merge automatically and commutatively. → Replication

docs.title: D–H
docs.text: Dead-man's switch — an always-firing signal whose silence is the alert. → Alerting Deadline propagation — remaining time-budget travels with the request; hops give up early. → Resilience DLQ (dead-letter queue) — quarantine for messages that exhaust retries; monitored, replayable. → Async fundamentals Envelope encryption — data encrypted with a DEK; DEK wrapped by a KMS-held KEK. → Secrets Erasure coding — k-of-n fragment redundancy: survive losses at ~1.5× overhead vs. 3× replication. → Object storage Error budget — the allowed failure implied by an SLO; spendable on velocity. → SLOs Event sourcing — the append-only event log as truth; state as a rebuildable fold. → Event-driven Expand–contract — schema/credential migration via add-alongside, dual-write, flip, remove-later. → SQL at scale Fail static — on control-plane loss, keep operating on last-known configuration. → K8s architecture Fan-out — one action becoming N operations (writes, calls); the amplification to count. → Thinking in systems Fencing token — monotonic token letting a resource reject stale lock-holders. → Coordination Gossip — peer-to-peer state dissemination; membership without a master. → KV store Gray failure — passing health checks while failing real work; the differential-observability failure. → Failure modes Head-of-line blocking — one stalled item blocking everything behind it (TCP, queues, partitions). → HTTP Hedged request — duplicate a laggard request; take the first answer; tame the tail. → Latency HLC (hybrid logical clock) — timestamps that look like wall time but respect causality. → Time & ordering Hot key/partition — one key's traffic exceeding its shard's capacity; the fleet average lies. → Cache failure modes

docs.title: I–P
docs.text: Idempotency — doing it twice equals doing it once; the license for retries. → Delivery semantics ISR (in-sync replicas) — Kafka's caught-up replica set; the durability quorum. → Kafka Jitter — randomized delay that desynchronizes herds (retries, TTLs, reconnects). → Resilience Lease — a lock with an expiry; the only sane distributed lock. → Coordination Linearizability — the single-copy illusion: reads see the latest completed write. → Consistency models Little's law — concurrency = throughput × latency; the equation you'll use forever. → Latency Load shedding — rejecting excess early and cheaply so accepted work completes. → Resilience LSM tree — write-optimized storage: memtable → immutable sorted files → compaction. → Storage engines Metastable failure — a system stuck broken after the trigger clears, sustained by a feedback loop. → Failure modes MTTR — mean time to recover; the availability lever you actually control. → Reliability MVCC — multi-version concurrency control: readers see snapshots, writers make versions. → Transactions Outbox pattern — event row committed in the business transaction; relay publishes after. → Distributed transactions PACELC — partitions force A-vs-C; else, latency-vs-consistency — the everyday tax. → CAP & PACELC Percentile (p99) — the value 99% of observations fall under; how latency is honestly spoken. → Latency Power of two choices — pick two random backends, send to the less loaded; near-optimal. → Load balancing

docs.title: Q–Z
docs.text: Quorum (R+W>N) — overlapping read/write sets guaranteeing reads see acknowledged writes. → Consistency models Raft — the understandable consensus protocol: terms, elections, replicated log. → Consensus Rate limiting — the front door's contract; token bucket and friends. → Rate limiting RED / USE — service metrics (rate, errors, duration) / resource metrics (utilization, saturation, errors). → Metrics Regional homing — each tenant's writes owned by a home region; conflicts impossible by assignment. → Multi-region RPO / RTO — how much data you may lose / how long recovery may take. → Multi-region Saga — distributed transaction as local steps + compensating actions. → Distributed transactions Shuffle sharding — per-customer random node subsets; blast radius shrinks combinatorially. → Reliability Sidecar — per-instance proxy carrying cross-cutting concerns (mTLS, retries, telemetry). → Service mesh SLI / SLO / SLA — measurement / target / contract-with-money. → SLOs Split brain — two nodes both believing they lead; divergent histories; why fencing exists. → Replication Stale-while-revalidate / stale-if-error — serve stale instantly while refreshing / when origin is down. → CDN Thundering herd / stampede — synchronized demand (expiry, reconnect, retry) crushing a target. → Cache failure modes Tombstone — a delete marker that must replicate before it can be forgotten. → KV store TrueTime — bounded-uncertainty clock intervals; commit-wait buys external consistency. → Time & ordering Vector clock — per-node counters detecting causality vs. concurrency. → Time & ordering WAL (write-ahead log) — append-first durability; the log the database is secretly made of. → Storage engines Watermark — a stream engine's claim that events older than T have (probably) arrived. → Event-driven Workload identity — attested, short-lived credentials replacing distributed secrets. → Secrets & identity Zero trust — authenticate every request, least privilege, assume breach. → Defense in depth

docs.title: Resources
docs.text: The short list that earns its hours — annotated with why and when, because a reading list without opinions is just a longer backlog.

docs.title: Books
docs.text:
• Designing Data-Intensive Applications — Martin Kleppmann. The canonical deep text; this site's data and distributed sections are the interview-ready layer above it. Read it after your first pass here, chapter-by-chapter alongside the matching sections. If you read one book, it's this one.
  • Site Reliability Engineering (the Google SRE book, free online) + The SRE Workbook. The source of SLOs, error budgets, and incident discipline. Skim the culture chapters, study the SLO/alerting/postmortem ones.
  • Understanding Distributed Systems — Roberto Vitillo. The gentlest rigorous on-ramp; excellent pre-DDIA if the theory pages here felt steep.
  • Database Internals — Alex Petrov. When storage engines hooked you and you want B-trees and consensus at implementation depth.
  • Release It! — Michael Nygard. The resilience patterns with production war stories attached; the book that named bulkheads and made timeouts a philosophy.
  • System Design Interview vol. 1 & 2 — Alex Xu. The interview-prep standard; breadth over depth. Use for question coverage, then come back here for the why under each answer.

docs.title: Papers (each ~an evening, each worth it)
docs.text:
• Dynamo (Amazon, 2007) — the availability-first thriller; read it after the KV-store case study and watch every mechanism click.
  • Raft: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Ongaro & Ousterhout) — readable as promised; pairs with the consensus page.
  • Spanner (Google, 2012) — TrueTime and commit-wait; the "pay latency for ordering" masterclass.
  • The Google File System + MapReduce — historical, foundational; where the big-data lineage begins.
  • Kafka: a Distributed Messaging System for Log Processing + Jay Kreps' essay "The Log" — the essay especially: the log-is-the-database worldview in its original voice; arguably the best single blog post in distributed systems.
  • Borg (Google, 2015) — Kubernetes' ancestor; read to see which K8s decisions were inherited scar tissue.
  • Shuffle Sharding (AWS builders' library) — blast-radius combinatorics in a short, superb read.
  • Metastable Failures in Distributed Systems (Bronson et al., HotOS '21) — the paper behind the concept that upgrades your incident vocabulary.

docs.title: Engineering blogs (the consistently-worth-it tier)
docs.text:
• AWS Builders' Library — Amazon's operational essays (timeouts and retries, shuffle sharding, static stability) — the closest thing to this site's ops callouts written by the people who coined them.
  • Cloudflare blog — networking depth (DNS, DDoS, protocol post-mortems) with real incident forensics.
  • Netflix TechBlog — chaos engineering, regional failover drills, adaptive concurrency.
  • Stripe engineering — idempotency, ledgers, API design; the payments case study's real-world footnotes.
  • Discord engineering — the chat case study at scale, repeatedly (their storage migrations are a series worth binging).
  • Uber engineering — geo-indexing (H3), matching, schemaless.
  • Meta engineering — cache consistency (the TAO and memcache papers/posts pair with caching), and MyRocks for the storage-engine swap story.
  • Incident write-up collections — public postmortems (Cloudflare's, Google's, GitHub's) are free chaos-engineering findings; read one per week and pattern-match against the failure-modes taxonomy.

docs.title: The leadership shelf
docs.text:
The companion canon for the Leadership & Strategy section — read alongside doing the job, not instead of it:

  • Staff Engineer — Will Larson. The Staff+ role defined: archetypes, getting the title, operating in it. The path page is the compressed version; this is the field guide. Pair with the free story collection at StaffEng.com.
  • The Staff Engineer's Path — Tanya Reilly. The complementary take: the "big picture, execution, leveling up" triad, and the best treatment in print of glue work and cross-team influence.
  • An Elegant Puzzle — Will Larson. Engineering management as systems thinking — read it even (especially) as an IC: it's the manual for the machine you're operating inside. The migrations chapter underwrites the strategy page.
  • Team Topologies — Skelton & Pais. The org-design vocabulary this site uses: four team types, three interaction modes, cognitive load as the sizing metric.
  • Accelerate — Forsgren, Humble & Kim. The research behind the DORA metrics — the evidence that delivery performance predicts business outcomes, i.e., the executive-craft page's footnotes.
  • Good Strategy / Bad Strategy — Richard Rumelt. The kernel (diagnosis → policy → action) that the strategy page borrows; the "bad strategy" chapters are a mirror most tech-strategy docs can't survive.
  • The Manager's Path — Camille Fournier. Even staying IC: it's the map of what your managers are supposed to be doing, which is exactly what you need to work with (and around) them at Staff+.
  • "Choose Boring Technology" — Dan McKinley (essay, free). Innovation tokens in the original voice; the single most quotable piece of technology strategy ever written.
  • High Output Management — Andy Grove. The 1983 book every modern management book is quietly rewriting: leverage, meetings as media, managerial output = output of the org beneath you.

docs.title: Talks
docs.text:
• "Mastering Chaos — A Netflix Guide to Microservices" (Josh Evans) — the microservice operational reality in one hour.
  • Raft visualizations (thesecretlivesofdata.com) — consensus animated; ten minutes, permanent intuition.
  • Kleppmann's "Distributed Systems" lecture series (Cambridge, free on YouTube) — when you want the theory pages as a taught course.

docs.title: How to use this list
docs.text: Interview-prep mode: this site → Alex Xu for question breadth → DDIA chapters for your weak sections. Depth mode (the Staff+ trajectory): DDIA cover to cover, one paper per week with notes, one public postmortem per week pattern-matched. The compounding trick either way: after every resource, write the three-sentence version in your own words — the expositor's rule: if you can't say it briefly, you don't own it yet.

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
Security in system design interviews is rarely a whole question and always a scoring surface: the candidate who authenticates their webhooks, scopes their tokens, and knows where the TLS terminates collects quiet points on every design, and at Staff level "how would this be attacked?" is a first-class review lens. Two pages cover what system design actually demands — identity (the part that appears in every design) and layered defense (the part that appears in every incident) — with secrets & workload identity already covered in the DevOps section as the third pillar.

  • Authentication & authorization

    Sessions vs. JWTs honestly, OAuth2/OIDC without the acronym fog, the revocation problem, and authorization models from RBAC to ReBAC.

  • Defense in depth

    Zero trust as an architecture, the layered model, DDoS and edge protection, data protection posture, and the secure-design checklist.

  • Secrets & identity (in DevOps & Platform)

    Vault/KMS, envelope encryption, workload identity, and rotation as engineering.

docs.title: If you are cramming
docs.text: Read the JWT-vs-session and revocation sections of authn-authz — they're the most probed security topics in system design — and the checklist at the end of defense in depth, which doubles as your review pass for any design you present.

docs.title: Authentication & Authorization
docs.text: Two questions gate every request in every system you'll ever design: who are you? (authentication) and what may you do? (authorization). The machinery answering them — sessions, JWTs, OAuth, RBAC — is where interviews probe security most, and where the probing has a favorite trap: the stateless-token story sounds elegant until someone asks how you log a user out. This page is the honest version, trap included.

docs.title: Sessions vs. JWTs: the stateful/stateless trade, again
docs.text:
Server-side sessions — login creates a record in a session store; the client holds an opaque cookie ID. Every request looks up the session. Properties: revocation is trivial (delete the record — logout, ban, and "sign out all devices" just work), sessions are inspectable and extendable, and the cost is a store lookup per request plus a stateful tier to operate (though a Redis hit is sub-millisecond — the "sessions don't scale" folklore predates modern KV stores and deserves retirement).

JWTs — the server signs a token containing the claims (user ID, roles, expiry); any service holding the public key verifies it locally — no lookup, no shared store, natural fit for multi-service fan-outs where the gateway validates once and passes verified identity inward. The price is the trap: a JWT is valid until it expires, full stop. Logout, permission downgrade, account compromise — the token doesn't know. Your options, all compromises: short expiry + refresh tokens (the standard: access token 5–15 min, revocable refresh token in secure storage — the dance below; compromise window shrinks to minutes), a denylist checked per-request (congratulations, you've rebuilt the session lookup), or accepting the window (fine for low-stakes claims, unacceptable for permission-bearing ones).

The senior synthesis, worth saying verbatim: JWTs for service-to-service and short-lived access; sessions (or session-backed refresh) where humans log out. Hybrid is the production norm: opaque session at the edge, JWT minted per-request for the interior hop chain (the gateway's identity-propagation job).

The refresh-token dance — the pattern behind every "stay logged in": short-lived access token + long-lived refresh token; refresh tokens are stored server-side (revocable!), single-use with rotation (each refresh issues a new one; reuse of an old one signals theft and kills the whole family — refresh-token-rotation-with-reuse-detection is the phrase that shows you know the current standard). JWT hygiene fine print that separates practitioners: validate alg (the none-algorithm attack is real history), short exp always, aud/iss checks (a token for service A must not work at service B), and keys rotated via JWKS endpoints (rotation as engineering, applied to signing keys).

docs.title: OAuth2 & OIDC without the fog
docs.text:
The acronyms unfold into two distinct jobs:

  • OAuth2 is delegated authorization: "let app X access my calendar without giving it my password." The flow that matters (authorization code + PKCE, which is now standard for every client type): app redirects you to the provider → you authenticate there and consent → app receives a one-time code → exchanges it (with proof) for tokens. The password never touches the app; the tokens are scoped (calendar-read, not everything) and revocable at the provider.
  • OIDC is authentication layered on OAuth2: adds the ID token (a JWT asserting who you are) — this is every "Sign in with Google/GitHub" button, and inside companies it's how SSO federates: your services never see passwords, only identity assertions from the IdP. (Workload identity federation — CI proving "I am repo X" for cloud credentials — is this exact machinery with machines as the users; one mental model, humans and workloads both.)

The scoping instinct generalizes everywhere and is a reliable senior signal: tokens carry the minimum capability (read:calendar, not *), short lifetimes, audience-bound — least privilege expressed in token claims.

docs.title: Authorization: the harder half
docs.text:
Authentication is a solved-ish protocol problem; authorization is a modeling problem that grows with your product:

  • RBAC — users → roles → permissions. Right default for internal tools and B2B tiers; strains when "editor" needs forty variants (role explosion — the cardinality problem, again).
  • ABAC — policies over attributes ("managers may approve expenses < $5k in their own department"). Expressive; auditable with discipline (policy-as-code engines — OPA-class — are ABAC with version control).
  • ReBAC — permissions as graph relationships ("viewer of doc X because member of group Y which is shared on folder Z") — the Google-Zanzibar lineage (SpiceDB-class), and the right model the moment your product has sharing, folders, or org hierarchies. Its system-design honesty: authorization becomes a graph query service on every request's critical path — cache it, replicate it, and mind the staleness-of-permissions trap (a revoked share that keeps working for 60 seconds is a security bug with a TTL).

Two placement rules that survive every model: enforce at the resource, hint at the edge (the gateway can reject the obviously-unauthorized, but the service owning the data makes the real decision — the fencing-token philosophy: the resource participates); and deny by default (authorization is an allowlist; every "fail open for convenience" becomes a finding).

DevOps lens

The operational surface: auth outages are total outages — the IdP/session store is tier-0 (cache validations with short TTLs so an IdP blip doesn't 401 the world — fail-static for identity, with the security-vs-availability window stated in writing); clock skew breaks tokens (the iat/exp incidents — "not yet valid" errors from one AZ = check NTP before the auth config); key rotation is a dual-validity dance (JWKS serves old+new during the roll, or every service 401s in the gap); and auth logs are your forensics — login attempts, token issuance, permission denials, rate-limited credential-stuffing patterns (auth endpoints get the strictest limits and fail closed). The incident genres: the mass logout (session store flush or signing-key mistake — every user, simultaneously, angry), the ghost permission (cached authz outliving revocation), and the federation dependency ("we can't log in because their IdP is down" — know your SSO blast radius).

Staff+ altitude

Markers: (1) One identity platform, not N — a single IdP/OIDC layer every service trusts, identity propagated via one header convention with verified provenance (the mesh's mTLS + JWT combo); five auth schemes is four too many attack surfaces, and consolidation is a Staff-year well spent. (2) Authorization as a product decision — the RBAC→ReBAC migration is driven by product shape (sharing! folders!), costs quarters, and is 10× cheaper anticipated than retrofitted; the Staff move is recognizing the product roadmap's authz implications before the sharing feature ships on if owner == user. (3) Token design is API design — claims schemas are contracts with all the versioning discipline that implies; the roles claim someone stuffed into the JWT in 2024 is load-bearing for forty services now. (4) Measure the security-latency budget — authz checks on every request are latency-budget line items; the Zanzibar-style answer (cache + async invalidation + bounded staleness, documented) is a worked trade-off, not a hand-wave.

In the interview

The set piece is the trap itself — deploy it before they spring it: "JWTs verified locally at each service for the interior; but revocation is the catch — a JWT is valid until expiry — so: 10-minute access tokens, rotating single-use refresh tokens stored server-side with reuse detection, and the session-revocation semantics live there." That's the modern standard in one breath. Probes: "how does Sign-in-with-Google work?" (OIDC on OAuth2: redirect, authenticate at provider, code + PKCE, exchange for ID token — password never touches you); "how do you do logout with JWTs?" (the honest menu: short expiry + revocable refresh; denylist if you must; "you've rebuilt sessions" said knowingly); "how would you model permissions for a Google-Docs-like product?" (ReBAC — relationships as a graph, Zanzibar lineage, and the ops honesty: it's a service on the critical path with caching and staleness trade-offs); "where do you enforce?" (edge hints, resource decides, deny by default). Security questions in system design are rarely deep — they're checking whether the defaults you reach for are the safe ones.

Next: Defense in depth — zero trust, the layered model, and the checklist that reviews any design in five minutes.

docs.title: Defense in Depth
docs.text: Every security architecture is a bet about failure — and the only bet that survives contact with reality is: any single control will eventually fail, so no single control may be load-bearing alone. Defense in depth is redundancy engineering applied to attack paths: independent layers, each catching what the previous missed, sized so that breaching one buys an attacker the next layer, not the crown jewels. This page is the layered model, the zero-trust reframe, the edge's special problems, and the five-minute checklist that security-reviews any design you'll ever present.

docs.title: Zero trust: the perimeter is dead, identity is the perimeter
docs.text:
The castle-and-moat model — hard shell, soft interior, "inside the VPN = trusted" — died of its own success stories: one phished credential or compromised pod inside the shell, and the flat interior network was a buffet. Zero trust is the replacement posture, and it's three sentences, not a product: every request is authenticated and authorized regardless of network origin (workload identity + mTLS everywhere — the mesh made it deployable); least privilege everywhere (identities carry minimum scopes, SG-to-SG rules name exact flows, the egress allowlist bounds what leaves); assume breach — design as if one component is already compromised and ask what it can reach (blast radius, attacker edition).

Note what zero trust did not do: delete the network layers. Isolated subnets, private endpoints, and segmentation still stand — as one more independent layer, no longer the only one. That's the depth in defense in depth.

docs.title: The layers, walked outside-in
docs.text:
• Edge — DDoS absorption at the CDN/anycast layer (volumetric attacks die in the provider's capacity, which exceeds yours by orders of magnitude), WAF for the signature-shaped attacks, rate limiting per key/IP with fail-closed auth endpoints, TLS termination with modern config. The design point interviews reward: cheap rejection early — the same philosophy as load shedding: every hostile request stopped at the edge never costs interior resources (and the penetration-attack/cache-miss genre is exactly this principle applied to fabricated keys).
  • Application — authn/authz enforced at the resource; input validation at every trust boundary (the OWASP evergreen trio in one sentence: parameterized queries kill injection, output encoding kills XSS, and never trust client-supplied identity/price/quantity — re-derive server-side); webhook signatures + replay windows; idempotency keys as fraud-dedup.
  • Platform — signed images admitted only, pipeline as trust boundary, secrets short-lived and attested, policy-as-code refusing public buckets before they exist. The platform layer is where one engineer's control protects four hundred repos — the paved road is a security architecture.
  • Data — encryption in transit (mTLS interior, TLS edge) and at rest (envelope encryption); crypto-shredding as the deletion story; classification driving handling (PII never in logs, residency by homing); and the unsung hero, immutability: object-lock on backups and audit trails — the ransomware defense and the compliance artifact in one control.
  • Detection & response — the layer that assumes the others failed: audit logs on an immutable, separate stream, anomaly alerting on auth patterns, flow logs enabled before the incident, and an incident process that treats "breach" as a rehearsed scenario (game days include security tabletops). Detection converts "compromised for nine months" into "compromised for ninety minutes" — at scale, that difference is the security program.

docs.title: Threat modeling, right-sized
docs.text: The heavyweight frameworks (STRIDE et al.) compress, for design-review purposes, into four questions asked of every arrow and box — the four questions' security twin: What's the asset here? (data, money, compute, reputation). Who's the attacker and what's their budget? (drive-by scanner ≠ organized fraud ≠ insider — controls scale to threat, not to paranoia). What's the path? (walk each trust boundary: client→edge, service→service, pipeline→prod, vendor→you — the boundary crossings are where controls live). What's the blast radius when this control fails? (assume breach, bound the damage). Fifteen minutes of this per design beats a hundred pages of policy nobody reads — and narrating it in an interview ("the asset is the payment token; the boundary crossing that worries me is the webhook from the processor; here's the signature check and replay window") is Staff-grade signal.

docs.title: The five-minute design review checklist
docs.text:
Run this against any architecture — yours, a colleague's, an interview whiteboard:

  1. Identity: every hop authenticated? Tokens scoped and short? Machine identities attested, not static?
  2. Authorization: enforced at the resource, deny-by-default, staleness of cached permissions bounded?
  3. Edge: rate limits (fail-closed on auth), DDoS story, input validation at first touch?
  4. Secrets: none in git/CI/env-baked images; rotation is dual-validity; cert expiry alarmed?
  5. Network: tiered subnets, SG-to-SG, egress allowlisted?
  6. Data: encrypted both states, classified, deletable on demand, absent from logs?
  7. Supply chain: images signed and admitted-by-policy, pipeline OIDC-federated?
  8. Detection: audit stream immutable and separate? Would you know within the hour?
  9. Blast radius: one compromised service — what can it reach? (The assume-breach walk.)
  10. The people path: break-glass audited? Offboarding revokes everything? (The insider is a threat model too.)

DevOps lens

Security operations is mostly hygiene at scale, and DevOps owns the machinery: patching as a pipeline (base-image rebuilds on CVE feeds, fleet-rolled with canary discipline — patch speed is a security SLO), posture as code (policy engines scanning for public buckets, permissive SGs, unencrypted volumes — findings auto-filed, drift treated as regression), the scanner triad (dependencies, images, IaC — in CI, blocking on criticals, with a flake-budget-style noise discipline so teams don't learn to ignore them), and credential-leak response as a rehearsed runbook (rotate-first-investigate-second; short-lived credentials shrink this from crisis to non-event — the best incident is the one whose stolen token expired an hour ago). The cultural note that makes it stick: security findings routed like error-budget burn — visible, owned, prioritized by the same machinery as reliability, not filed in a parallel universe nobody visits.

Staff+ altitude

Markers: (1) Security is a property of defaults — the Staff-level insight is that org-wide security lives in the paved road (the template with mTLS on, the pipeline with signing built in, the bucket module that can't go public), not in review vigilance; every control moved from "remembered" to "inherited" is permanent risk reduction. (2) Threat-model the org, not just the app — the pipeline, the flag system, the IdP, the laptop fleet: the highest-leverage attacks target the systems that control systems, and the architecture review that skips them reviewed the wrong architecture. (3) Compliance is a by-product of good design — SOC 2/ISO controls map almost 1:1 onto this page plus secrets and audit logging; build the platform right and the audit is evidence-gathering, not engineering (the same claim as secrets, because it keeps being true). (4) Risk acceptance is a written decision — "we accept the 60-second permission-staleness window because the alternative costs 15 ms per request" is legitimate when documented and owned; silent risk acceptance is just risk, and the Staff artifact is the register where these trades live.

In the interview

Security scoring in design interviews is mostly defaults and reflexes — collect the points as you go: TLS assumed everywhere, tokens scoped and short, webhooks signed, rate limits with fail-closed auth, secrets attested not stored, buckets private by policy. Then one deliberate set piece when the design stabilizes — the assume-breach walk: "if this worker is compromised: it holds a 15-minute scoped token, reaches only the queue and its own bucket path via egress allowlist, can't see other tenants' keys, and its anomalous S3 listing shows in flow logs within minutes — blast radius is one tenant-hour of data." That paragraph is defense in depth demonstrated, and it's more convincing than any recitation of layers. Probes: "how do you protect against DDoS?" (edge absorption + cheap early rejection + the priority-shedding continuum — same machinery, hostile traffic); "what's zero trust actually?" (three sentences, not a vendor: authenticate every request, least privilege, assume breach — then note the network layers remain as depth); "how do you handle a leaked credential?" (rotate-first runbook + the structural answer: short-lived attested credentials make the question small).
